[[RunningHead]]

Any Wife or Any Husband

By Medica (Dr. Joan Malleson). (Messrs. William Heinemann, Medical Books, Ltd. Price 7 s. 6 d.)

S. Yates

S. Yates

S. Yates  

This is a wise, well-written book, obviously the fruit of much clinical experience. It deals with the sexual problems of marriage, their origin and treatment.

The book is written in a clear, practical, interesting way, without any medical jargon, but, nevertheless, the matter is all based on sound psycho-analytic principles. It is warmly recommended to general practitioners and laymen, especially to Marriage Guidance Councillors, etc., to help them in the advice they themselves give, especially with reference to when a gynæcologist should be consulted and when a psychiatrist, and so on.

One of the outstanding features of the book is the way 'Medica' emphasises the large range of sexual behaviour which can be included in the normal, a fact which is often overlooked.

responseSet.title: Preface
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Preface

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones  

I have in previous Prefaces to Mrs. Klein's writings expressed my estimation of her scientific work, which, therefore, need not be repeated here. The present occasion, however, is a more personal one. We are united in our birthday wishes to her, but I feel sure that to Mrs. Klein their value is far transcended by another matter for congratulation that can be associated with them. It is that she has lived to see her work firmly established. So long as it was simply deposited in what she herself had published there was always the hope, but by no means the certainty, that it would be taken up by future students. Now the situation is far more favourable. It has moved beyond that possibly static stage. As a result of her personal instruction, combined with the insight of those who decided to accept it, she has a considerable number of colleagues and pupils who follow her lead in exploring the deepest depths. To the papers that many of them contributed on this happy occasion I have the pleasure of adding this envoi.

Figure 1. MELANIE KLEIN

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Brain and Personality

By Paul Schilder. (New York: International Universities Press, 1951. Pp. 136. London: Bailey Bros. 20 s.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel
London

E. Stengel  

It can justly be said of Schilder that he explored the problems of the body-mind relationship more fully and more persistently than any of his contemporaries. His vast knowledge in many fields, including psycho-analysis, made him the ideal integrator.

In this book two series of lectures are presented. The first, on the psychological aspects of cerebral neuropathology, was given to the Washington Psycho-analytic Group; the second, on neuropsychiatric aspects of the motility of schizophrenics, to the staff of the Henry Phipps Psychiatric Institute. Both series were given in 1928 and 1929 in Schilder's first year in the U.S.A. as a visiting member of Professor Adolf Meyer's staff. This book was previously published in 1931 as No. 53 of the Nervous and Mental Disease Monograph Series.

To Schilder the main purpose of a lecture was not to transmit information but to stimulate thought. His listeners felt themselves in the presence of a fertile mind thinking aloud. He was not much concerned with the art of exposition and unashamed of the imperfections of knowledge. The lectures here collected reflect Schilder's manner of teaching very well. The first series is entitled 'Studies in the Psychological Aspects of Cerebral Neuropathology'. The first lecture deals with sleep and consciousness. This and the following chapters contain many illustrations of the mutual influence of organic and psychic processes. In 'Problems of Tonus' the rôle of the vestibular apparatus in the building-up of the body scheme and the effects of abnormal vestibular stimuli on consciousness and dreams are discussed. The third lecture deals with the psychopathology of encephalitis and the types of regression observed in neuroses and in cases with brain lesions. Regression is regarded as always due to the same basic mechanism taking place on various levels of integration. Brain lesion and mental conflict result in similar types of behaviour disorder. In visual agnosia and aphasia the primary process as described by Freud can be demonstrated in action.

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 1

'Toward Unification of Training in Psychiatry and Psychoanalysis.' Howard W. Potter, M.D., Brooklyn, N.Y., and Henriette R. Klein, M.D., New York, N.Y. The American Journal of Psychiatry, 108, No. 3, September, 1951, pp. 193–197.

Martin Grotjahn M.D.

Martin Grotjahn

Martin Grotjahn  

Psychiatric residents indicated in a questionnaire as used by the two authors a need for organized training in psycho-dynamics, psychiatry, and psycho-analysis throughout their residencies. Observers could make no valid comparisons between the professional competence of residents who were in training at psycho-analytic institutes and those not being similarly trained. Residents in training at analytic institutes felt that this did not set up restrictions or limitations nor interfere with their clinical work but, rather, operated in a positive way during the residency training. All the teaching psychiatrists and all the teaching psycho-analysts agree that organized instruction in psychodynamics should be given in the residency training centre. Opinions about the value of a personal analysis independent of training for the practice of psycho-analysis were found to be based on tradition or personal experience rather than on investigative studies. The general consensus of teaching analysts was that there could be no modification of personal analysis of residents as to depth, breadth, or time. Half the analysts who recommended that residents undertake a personal analysis stated that it should begin at the start of the psychiatric residency or before.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 1: Merton M. Gill.  'Ego Psychology and Psychotherapy.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 1

Merton M. Gill. 'Ego Psychology and Psychotherapy.'

Various problems of psychotherapy are discussed from the standpoint of ego psychology.

1. Indications. The choice between psycho-analysis and psychotherapy is determined by the therapeutic goal that is thought desirable or practicable in any particular case. This depends on various factors, including the reality situation, but fundamentally it is a matter of deciding whether

75

the defences of the ego should be strengthened in order to reinstate the position prevailing before the neurotic breakdown or whether they should be broken through in order to achieve a re-integration of the ego. Psychotherapy is indicated when strengthening the ego is all that is necessary. Analysis is indicated in a middle range of cases in which the ego is sufficiently damaged to require repair but remains sufficiently strong for defences to be analysed without risking disintegration of the ego. Analysis plus supportive therapy is indicated in borderline cases, where there is a danger of impairing what little integration the ego still retains. Cases in which symptoms and character disorders have become ego-syntonic also demand analysis.

2. Techniques. Various principles underlying the techniques of strengthening defences are formulated. (i) Ego activities in which defence is combined with adaptive gratification should be encouraged, while those which are maladaptive should be discouraged. (ii) Important defences should not be attacked unwittingly. (iii) Remote derivatives of defensive systems that are not being used maladaptively should be ignored. (iv) The theory of strengthening defences can be formulated in terms of Glover's suggestion that artificial neuroses may be set up which offer partial discharge for instinct derivatives, and thereby decrease the amount of defensive work demanded of the ego.

3. Hypnosis. Earlier work by the present writer and his co-workers has shown that the view that defences are abolished by hypnosis is incorrect. The defence mechanism of repression is often altered, but it is uncertain whether or to what extent other defences are changed. Hypnosis is more correctly seen as a state of altered ego function than as one in which defences are obliterated. The ego's capacity to reflect and synthesize can remain relatively unimpaired and subjects in deep hypnosis can examine the hypnotic state itself and deal with its transference implications. It appears that the analysis of the transference does not make further hypnosis impossible, nor does it undo symptomatic improvements.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 1

Milton L. Miller. 'The Traumatic Effect of Surgical Operations in Childhood on the Integrative Functions of the Ego.'

Clinical material is presented from the analyses of two patients who as small children had had surgical operations. In each case pre-existing Oedipal anxiety and guilt about masturbation led to the operation having a traumatic effect, producing phobic symptoms and character defences. Both patients responded unconsciously to competitive situations in adult life as though to the threat of an operation, and the repetitive nature of this reaction constituted a problem. Therapeutic progress in each case was the result of uncovering repressed emotions associated with the operation and interpreting the way in which the ego-defences used to deal with current problems were a repetition of those used to deal with the infantile trauma.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 1

Leon J. Saul. 'Two Observations on the Split in Object Choice.'

Charles Rycroft

Charles Rycroft

Charles F. Rycroft
London

Charles Rycroft  

The general split in the sexual object choice in men, and their difficulty in fusing sensual and tender feelings, are discussed in relation to the man's dependence on his mother and his need to find an outlet for his hostility. These two factors are often

76

interconnected, since dependence provokes hostility as a result of the frustrations and narcissistic injuries involved. The sexual interest in depreciated women provides an outlet not only for active, sexual wishes that are incompatible with passive, dependent longings, but also for hostility which cannot be directed towards the idealized mother or mother-substitute. The depreciated woman is identified with neither the mother nor the patient himself, who therefore feels free to attack her. Since hostility is probably a biological element in sexuality, some guilt, conscious or unconscious, is probably unavoidable.

77

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

General Theory of Neuroses

Twenty-two lectures on the Biology, Psycho-analysis, and Psycho-hygiene of Psychosomatic Disorders. By Rudolf Brun. (New York: International University Press, 1951. Pp. 468.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

Professor Brun has long been known as a distinguished scholar in several fields. His studies on the instincts of the ants won him early recognition. He has also made important contributions to neurology and psychiatry. There have been three great influences in his career: August Forel, Monakow, and Freud. In this book he attempts to synthesize his masters' concepts. The result is an impressive work characterized by the thoroughness, sincerity, and lucidity which are the hallmarks of Swiss scholarship.

The material is presented in lecture form. There are twenty-two lectures in all. The author proposes a new clinical classification. The neuroses are a group among the 'primary disorders of the instinctive life' called the hormopathies, which may be organic or functional in origin. the former fall into three groups: the dysglandular, the constitutional endogenous, i.e. the major psychoses, and the organic cerebral. The functional hormopathies are identical with the neuroses which, again, are divided into two groups according to their supposed causation. The first group consists of the vegetative neuroses (to which Freud's actual neurosis belongs), the neurasthenias, and the fright neuroses. They are believed to be due to mechanical or toxic disturbances of the vegetative cerebral centres. The nature of those hypothetical disturbances is still obscure. The second group are the psychoneuroses proper (Freud's transference neuroses), i.e. the hysterias, the phobias, and the obsessional neuroses. They are truly psychogenic. That differentiation of the neuroses is the author's main thesis and he is quite dogmatic about it. He believes that Monakow's 'plexus theory' throws light on the etiology of the first group, which also includes anxiety neurosis. According to Monakow, the functions of the brain can be impaired by mechanical or toxic damage to the hemoencephalic barrier of which the plexus chorioidei are the chief part. It is only fair to point out that no agreement has been reached among neuropathologists about the significance of the findings on which Monakow based his far-reaching speculations on the causation of mental disorders.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2: Max M. Stern.  'Anxiety, Trauma and Shock.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

Max M. Stern. 'Anxiety, Trauma and Shock.'

Correlating the basic processes of 'adaptive stabilization' (homeostasis) with analytical concepts, a physiological shock theory of anxiety is introduced as an operational hypothesis. Stress of any kind produces two systemic reactions, shock, which is the approach of death in natural ways, and the homoeostatic reaction (shock defence), due mainly to endocrine activity (adrenals, hypophysis). Shock from psychic over-stimulation is the consequence of a psychic traumatic situation (rupture of the stimulus barrier) when the homoeostatic defence functions inadequately.

Anxiety is defined as an anticipatory reproduction of traumatic situations experienced in early infancy, i.e. of the shock reaction which, owing to the immaturity of the postnatal organism, is physiological in the postnatal period and, to a lesser extent (paralleling ego maturation), throughout the early infantile period. The concept of anxiety as an anticipatory reproduction of shock leads, by means of a detailed investigation into the processes connected with physiological shock, to a surprising clarification of a number of basic psychic phenomena such as rage, fright, pavor nocturnus, repression, depersonalization, depression, mania, patterns of anxiety, etc. They are represented as partial or entire anticipatory reproductions of biological reactions which, at the beginning of life, guaranteed survival by warding off shock.

As muscular action intensifies the homoeostatic response, increased muscular activity, the prerequisite of fight and flight, is seen as a physiological defence mechanism against impending shock (hypothalamic defence). The increased motility which invalidates the threat of impending shock in the infant is the biological basis for the rage reaction, the prototype of aggressive activity. Fright corresponds to a transitory shock reaction, elicited by too sudden over-stimulation, which is immediately followed by a violent defence reaction (palpitation of the heart, heavy breathing).

Three different phases are observed in the infant's response to stress: the phase of excitation, the catatonoid phase and the phase of shock. In these responses, efferent reactions of defence against shock are progressively mixed with paralysis from shock which, unchecked, often leads to death (marasmus, hospitalism). Biologically, the catatonoid reaction is the last line of defence against shock. Thus, psychotic phenomena, like catatonia, depression, depersonalization, and the like are defined as defence mechanisms against overwhelming anxiety. A catatonoid reaction also occurs in fright ('freezing').

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2: Otto E. Sperling.  'Illusions, Naive or Controlled.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

Otto E. Sperling. 'Illusions, Naive or Controlled.'

An illusion is naive if the deception either of our senses or of our minds is complete and not contradicted. On the other hand, if we permit ourselves to give in to this illusion only to a certain degree and for a limited time, and retain the ability to recognize that this is an illusion, or if we are oscillating between belief and disbelief, we are dealing with what the author would like to term a 'controlled illusion'. In the same way that we speak of frigidity or impotence in sex, so we have a right to speak of a frigidity in the arts, music, or the beauties of nature. The impoverishment of the ego in these cases is expressed by an inability to form illusions. Controlled illusions are a necessary lubricant in our civilization. They are even more important in the transference relationship, in the love of children, in adult love, and in euthanasia. Naive illusions lead to traumatic disenchantment, or to conflicts with the environment. The cases of a religious fanatic and of a Nazi are presented from the point of view of naive illusions.

It is a naive illusion to believe in absolute values. The individual derives his intrinsic values from the id and from the super-ego. Instrumental values—work, schooling, money—are characteristic of the operations of the ego. In so far as traditions and social institutions are integrated in the super-ego, there are a great number of similarities in the systems of values of people of similar origin. Yet many yield to the illusion that their system is the right one, that their values were discovered and not awarded.

If parents present their values as absolute and their children are confronted with a different system of values in school, delinquency, a continuous conflict, or a controlled illusion about the local value of both systems might ensue.

A scientist cannot be free of a value system, but he can have controlled illusions about its absoluteness. Controlled illusions are a relatively safe compromise between the reality principle and the pleasure principle. They correspond in the emotional sphere to the fictions of science or philosophy in the sphere of thinking. The addition of illusions to instinct gratifications is an example of the crowding of emotions as an expression of the life instinct in a tendency towards intensification of pleasure.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2: Leo Stone.  'Psychoanalysis and Brief Psychotherapy.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

Leo Stone. 'Psychoanalysis and Brief Psychotherapy.'

The author first attempts to establish a functional basis for the separation of psycho-analysis from other psychotherapeutic techniques, especially those which resemble it closely and which are usually based on extrapolations from it. The reasons for the desirability of this distinction are:

1. Technical variables may alter mechanisms often assumed to remain the same.

2. The development of clear-cut technical categories, with corresponding therapeutic indications, beginning with psycho-analysis itself, would be clinically advantageous.

3. Psycho-analysis should be preserved as the acknowledged scientific source for psychotherapy in general.

4. The requirements of training analysis.

Mention is made of the paradoxical position of brief psychotherapy in clinical practice and psycho-analytic training, especially of the fact that the variations from well-defined psycho-analytic technique are usually delegated to untrained or partially trained physicians. Brief critical mention is made of the extensive contributions of the Chicago Institute. The need for re-evaluation and exploitation of all psychotherapeutic methods to meet the enormous present need for treatment by all social groups is stated. The importance of a tentative broad psycho-analytic interpretation of the personality and illness, as a prerequisite of the rational employment of any therapeutic method, is stressed. Aside from the numbers of patients who cannot have psycho-analysis because of problems of time or money, there are many for whom positive indications for less intensive techniques exist. The author offers several such categories, based on his own opinions and those of other writers, ranging from those patients whose illness or life situation are too unpropitious to those whose illness is too mild, including special categories, such as certain reactive disorders, or those connected with transitional life situations.

Brief mention is made of an incipient inquiry in the New York Institute regarding the effects of technical variations. The author also states the most frequent tendencies in his own personal approach to eight typical psychotherapeutic situations.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

Bernice Engle and Thomas M. French. 'Some Psychodynamic Reflections upon the Life and Writings of Solon.'

Charles Rycroft

Charles Rycroft

Charles Rycroft  

The life and writings of Solon are discussed from the point of view of the light they can throw on some psychodynamic problems of democracy.

Solon was appointed archon of Attica at the beginning of the sixth century B.C. with extraordinary powers to reconstruct the machinery of the Athenian government which had broken down as a result of the disintegrating effect of commercial development on a previously feudal society. What had once been a benevolent aristocracy had become an oligarchy of limitless greed. In their discussion of his reforms, which included the abolition of slavery and extension of civic rights, the authors stress the high value Solon attached to self-restraint as a counterbalance to the lust for power and wealth. This is exemplified both in his writings and in his refusal to seize arbitrary power for himself and become a tyrant. If a democracy is to work its members must not only be capable of resisting despotic usurpation of power; they must also be willing to renounce their own desire for power. Solon is one of the earliest examples of such self-restraint.

The fact that Solon had to defend himself against charges of weakness on account of his refusal to seize power for himself 'illustrates a psychodynamic dilemma which confronts every democracy'. The success of democracy depends upon the maintenance of a delicate balance between excessive and insufficient inhibition of self-assertive impulses. 'A community whose members are too ready to surrender their individual interests to the general welfare becomes only too ready to succumb to the usurpations of the next tyrant.

505

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

Sidney J. Baker. 'Autonomic Resistances in Word Association Tests.'

Leon Altman

Leon Altman

Leon Altman  

Baker inquires into the meaning and purpose of common hesitative interjections in the speech of testees responding to word association tests. Paraphasias, deviations and disturbances in the stream of speech such as, 'ha', 'ah', 'um', 'er', 'hmm' are interpreted as marginal linguistic resistances. Resistance in the responses of testees is also betrayed by extra-linguistic mannerisms and acts such as fidgeting, scratching, yawning, belching, coughing, sighing, laughing, borborygms and passage of flatus, as well as by the use of temporal delay. This has been noted by other analysts. Ferenczi remarked on talkativeness as a resistance and Freud recognized his patient's (Dora's) dyspnoea, coughing and aphonia as 'somatic statements' which were guides to her anxiety and resistances. Both groups of acts, the marginal linguistic (paraphasias) and the extra-linguistic, are considered involuntary processes linked by common innervation by the autonomic nervous systems.

Baker suggests that the aim of this kind of 'speech' is an aggressive attempt to resolve an intrapersonal conflict. It is designed to restore the testee's disturbed equilibrium by silencing the tester and attaining the tension-free silence which is 'the unconscious aim of all speech'.

505

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

J. O. Wisdom. 'The Hypothesis of Cybernetics.' The British Journal for the Philosophy of Science, 11, 1951, pp. 1–24.

This is a critical exposition of cybernetics. The nature of the feed-back mechanisms is explained in detail and several types of feed-back distinguished. The hypotheses of cybernetics are stated to be (i) 'that the chief mechanism of the central nervous system is one of negative feed-back' (p. 5), and (ii) that the negative feed-back mechanism explains 'purposive' and 'adaptive' behaviour. The latter is illustrated by a radar-controlled gun. Several kinds of application and confirmation are mentioned and discussed, e.g. the oscillations displayed in cerebellar disease are interpreted as effects of undamped feed-back mechanisms in the central nervous system; a homoeostat is described which is capable of reorganizing itself when interfered with; the possibility that the scansion mechanism of television is the mechanism of vision is discussed; closed circuits of neurons reverberating like electronic tubes (and other analogies) are mentioned. Attention is drawn to von Bertalanffy's theory of the organism as an 'open system' and to electro-encephalography on account of their bearing on cybernetics. In the concluding evaluation it is suggested that cybernetics leaves out the mind and is in the tradition of physiology and neurology. A bibliographical note is attached.

Author's Abstract.

506

Conference Des Psychanalystes De Langues Romanes. This Conference will take place at the Clinique des Maladies Mentales et de L'Encéphale, Centre Psychiatrique Sainte-Anne, 1, rue Cabanis, Paris, under the auspices of the Société Psychanalytique de Paris on November 9 and 10, 1952. Dr. S. Nacht will be in the chair.

Topics for discussion: (1) Theoretical —Dr. M. Benassy on Theories of Instincts. (2) Clinical —Dr. M. Bouvet on The Ego in Obsessional Neuroses. Object Relationship and Mechanisms of Defence. Information from the Conference Secretary: Dr. Pierre Marty, 22 Boulevard Barbès, Paris 18.

Testimonial Dinner Tendered Dr. Clarence P. Oberndorf. A testimonial dinner in honour of Dr. Clarence P. Oberndorf was given on February 15, the eve of his seventieth birthday, at the Waldorf Astoria Hotel, New York. The dinner, attended by 350 guests, was arranged by a group of Dr. Oberndorf's close friends under the chairmanship of Drs. Dudley Schoenfeld and Sandor Lorand. Prominent among those who gathered were many leaders in the psychiatric and psycho-analytic field, co-workers of Dr. Oberndorf in his varied psychiatric and community activities and many other friends, including alumni of his college.

The spirit of celebration and appreciation for the guest of honour was heartily expressed by Dr. Ralph M. Kaufman, who acted as Toastmaster, and the speakers, who included Dr. Leo Bartemeier, Dr. Bernard Gluek and Mr. I. Howard Lehman.

The early pioneering work of Dr. Oberndorf in helping to introduce psycho-analysis in the United States was recalled by Dr. Bartemeier. Continuing the appraisal of the guest of honour's work, Dr. Gluek stressed his sense of responsibility as contributor and leader in psychiatry and psycho-analysis. Dr. Oberndorf's contribution to the community, especially in the field of caring for deprived children, was eloquently presented by Mr. Lehman, former President of the Jewish Child Care Association of New York. Dr. Marion E. Kenworthy graciously gave the toast of the evening.

responseSet.title: Stages in the Development of Control Over Fire
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Stages in the Development of Control Over Fire

Alexander Grinstein M.D.

Alexander Grinstein

Alexander Grinstein, M.D.
DETROIT, MICH

Alexander Grinstein  

An analysis of the concept of control over fire implies that man must be able to do the following: (1) conserve fire while it is burning so that it does not go out, (2) confine it to a particular locale so that it does not spread and destroy either the surrounding property or himself; (3) extinguish it when he is through with its use; and (4) start it once more at will. Only when all these conditions have been fulfilled, can it be truly said that man has control over fire.

In his paper on 'The Acquisition of Power over Fire', (1) Freud elaborated the thesis which he had expressed earlier in a footnote in Civilization and its Discontents(2) that, 'in order to possess himself of fire, it was necessary for man to renounce the homosexually tinged desire to extinguish it with a stream of urine'.

Using Freud's paper as a point of departure for further investigation into the problems involved in the acquisition of the control over fire, a number of questions arise: (1) What force or forces were responsible for the renunciation of the urge to urinate on the fire? (2) Are there any pregenital or preurethral controls which were associated with the development of the control over fire, suggested by our general knowledge of the stages of libidinal development? (3) What psychical factors were responsible for man's learning the secret of starting a fire?

To find a solution to these problems is extremely difficult because of the nature of the material with which we have to deal. Our conclusions, moreover, at best can only be constructions based upon primitive mythology and some observations from the case material of the analysis of adult neurotic patients. From the myths we learn that in order to possess himself of fire it was necessary for man to obtain it by trick, or aggressive action of some kind from another person (often represented as an animal or bird with human attributes). Freud, in his analysis of similar material, particularly that found in the myth of Prometheus, stated that actually the god who is defrauded when the gratification of extinguishing fires (by urination) is renounced is none other than the id.

responseSet.title: An Obsessional Man's Need to be 'Kept'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Obsessional Man's Need to be 'Kept'

Beryl Sandford

Beryl Sandford

Beryl Sandford
LONDON

Beryl Sandford  

The patient about whom I am writing, and whom I will call Mr. A, seemed, at the beginning of his analysis four years ago, to suffer from practically every known neurotic symptom. And, since he produced analytic material as lavishly as he produced symptoms, I have had some ado to condense this paper to a reasonable length and, at the same time, make it comprehensible.

He is a typical obsessional with marked paranoid and agoraphobic features, and with a neurosis of such long standing that it gives the impression of having become part of his total personality. In this paper I am going to discuss one particular aspect of his case, and that is his need to be 'kept'. This does not mean that he cannot work; it means that he cannot work for money.

Mr. A, who is a Clinic patient, comes from a superior working class family, and is now aged forty-one. Before the war he had had three years psychotherapy, but this had to be stopped because of the outbreak of war. Mr. A was exempted from all forms of National Service because of his mental illness.

He is a fine figure of a man, about six foot tall and broad in proportion. His hair is black and brilliantined. He has a magnificent speaking voice, and used to lie on the couch declaiming flamboyantly as though acting in Victorian melodrama.

Everything about him, both mental and physical, seemed to give the impression of being a little larger than life. He talked in rounded periods. He alluded to anyone who was hard up as being 'financially embarrassed'; if he owed someone eighteen-pence he talked of a 'pecuniary obligation'; and anyone of average intelligence was always said to have a 'brilliant intellect'.

His mother, who is still alive, was a children's nurse before she married. She appears to share many of her son's contamination fears. His father, who was a builder, died of Tb. at the beginning of the war. Mr. A is the youngest of three children—his sister and brother being twins, two years older than he is. Both, according to Mr. A have 'brilliant intellects'. The brother, who is married but childless, is a chartered surveyor earning good money; the sister is married to a factory inspector and has four children.

[[RunningHead]]

Recent Progress in Psychiatry

Published by authority of the Royal Medico-Psychological Association, Journal of Mental Science. (London: J. and A. Churchill Ltd. Second Edition. 1951. 50 s.)

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

The first review under this title was published in 1944. This edition covers more or less the same topics, but contains 200 more pages.

The chapters which will interest psycho-analysts most are 'Psychotherapy' by E. A. Bennet; 'Suicide' by E. Stengel; 'Psychopathology' by S. M. Coleman; 'Cybernetics' by W. R. Ashby. The review of 'Personality Tests 1944–49' by H. J. Eysenck; 'Schizophrenia' by H. Gillies; 'Delinquency and Crime' by Sir Norwood East; 'Sleep and its Disturbances' by M. N. Pai are also relevant to psycho-analytic work.

In the Introduction, G. W. T. H. Fleming, editor of the Journal of Mental Science, promises a third edition in 1955, in which some of the topics omitted in the second edition but covered in the first, for instance Child Psychiatry, will be included. He mentions psychiatric abstracts, such as Excerpta Medica, as being now indispensable, but does not discuss the relationship of the type of review published in this book to abstracting journals.

Although the reviews are of unequal value in their discriminating comment and inclusiveness, they will be of great value to many; nevertheless some readers will look forward to the time when a body such as the Royal Medico-Psychological Association can foster reviews in the psychiatric field similar, for example, to those published in psychological or physiological reviews.

61

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to a Psychoanalytic Psychiatry

By Paul Schilder. Trans. by Bernard Glueck. (New York: Inter. Universities Press, Inc., 1951. Pp. ix + 178. London: Bailey Bros. 26 s.

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

Schilder's death as the result of a street accident at the early age of fifty-four in 1940 left a sorry gap in the group of psycho-analytic psychiatrists who constantly stimulated American students. It will be many years before the value of Schilder's publications becomes merely historical.

61

The book now under review was first translated and published in 1928 as No. 50 of the Nervous and Mental Disease Monograph series a few years after its publication in German. Schilder's was the first attempt to apply meta-psychology to psychiatry on a wide basis and, for this reason alone, the book should be read by all students of psycho-analysis.

Although the contributions psycho-analysts have made to the psychopathology of the functional psychoses during the past twenty-five years supersede much of what Schilder has here written, he asks many questions which are still unanswered, and too few writers stimulate students by asking the questions to which there are still no answers.

His chapters on amentia, aphasia, agnosia, epilepsy, paresis, Korsakoff disorder and deliria cover fields still neglected by psycho-analysts.

The re-publication of this book is very welcome.

62

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 1: Ives Hendrick.  'Early Development of the Ego:  Identification in Infancy.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 1

Ives Hendrick. 'Early Development of the Ego: Identification in Infancy.'

Earlier papers have shown that the pathology of the psychoses and also of many character disorders is the result of defects in ego development, and that the symptoms are end-results not of a healthy ego's defences against unresolved infantile conflicts but of a fundamental inadequacy of the ego itself. This conception of 'ego-defect' neuroses demands a more comprehensive analysis of normal ego development than has yet been attempted, and forces us to realize that studies of the ego have been largely concerned with defence mechanisms that presuppose an ego of considerable maturity. This paper is concerned with the impact of emotional and realistic forces from the environment on the development of 'partial ego-functions' and in particular with the causes and effects of identification in infancy.

Identification originates in the wish to be like another individual in some way and leads to the assimilation of the desired traits as stable and permanent elements of the personality. Identifications which contribute to basic ego development result from much earlier and more primitive relations of the infant with its object than do those which lead to super-ego formation. They involve partial rather than total cathexes of the object and are responses to frustration of primitive needs rather than to prohibitions resulting from highly complex object relations. In contrast to super-ego identifications they arise from a two-person relationship, being the result of ambivalence towards one object, the mother.

Although ego-identification is presumably initiated by pleasure in imitation, significant and lasting identifications are the result of ambivalent relationships and are related to envy of the power of the frustrating object. It can be assumed, therefore, that the infant's first identifications occur only when there is some kind of recognition of external objects as such and some kind of awareness of frustration by them. This presupposes a certain development of the discriminating powers of the special senses and some perception of recurrent internal tensions and of their recurrent pleasurable relief in association with partial perceptions of the mother. As soon as association of some aspect of the mother with anticipation of gratification has taken place, motor activities such as cries of hunger cease to be simply responses to need and become emotional demands.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mind, Perception and Science

By W. Russell Brain. (Oxford: Blackwell, 1951. Pp. vi + 90. 6 s.)

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom  

Of recent years certain neurologists have displayed an increased interest in questions of consciousness and philosophical problems of perception. Sir Russell Brain has found that neurology does not give answers to his questions and that the answers offered by philosophers do not altogether satisfy him. He has therefore had to philosophize for himself. In this Manson Lecture, here reprinted, he gives some excellent criticism of the classical neurological account of perception. He lays stress on the rôle of the body, and he thinks that the 'body-image' must play a fundamental part in the explanation, for instance, of localization of sensations and of our awareness of externality. He moves away from the old atomistic conception and regards patterns as basic; he regards states of mind as influenced by patterns of neural impulses.

This may show to what an extent Sir Russell Brain is trying to question common assumptions within neurology. When we come to the positive reconstruction that he attempts to make, however, the result is rather thin, and it is not at all clear what precisely is the theory of perception he is putting forward. His answer to the question, for instance, how the nervous system can yield information about the external world is that the nervous system provides symbolic representation, i.e. provides symbols both for external objects, and for relations between them, and also for relations between them and the body. The concept of symbolic representation would require a great deal of elaboration before we could judge whether it could carry such a load. None the less, whatever the deficiencies of the theory, it is useful to know how an eminent neurologist views such matters. Provided solutions are not expected, parts of the book should be widely read for the very stimulating things they contain.

[[RunningHead]]

Cybernetics

Transactions of the Sixth Conference, 1950. Transactions of the Seventh Conference, 1951. Edited by H. von Foerster. (Josiah Macy Jr. Foundation, New York. $3.50 each.)

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom  

The United States is fortunate in having such Foundations and men of imagination and means to bring them into being.

This Foundation has at least thirteen conference groups, of which cybernetics is one. On account of the wide ramifications of the subject, experts from many different fields constitute the group. The proceedings are informal. Nothing was printed from the first five meetings. But the discussions at the sixth and seventh were recorded and are published in these two volumes. The topics discussed in the sixth were: 'Psychological Moment in Perception'; 'Neurotic Potential and Human Adaptation'; 'Quantum Theory of Memory'; 'Possible Mechanisms of Recall and Recognition'; 'Sensory Prothesises'. The seventh opened with 'Some of the Problems concerning Digital Notions in the Central Nervous System', and then devoted all the other discussions to aspects of language, the relation of which to cybernetics is not clear.

It is impossible to review the content of such work. The books make interesting reading and they continually stimulate. Their great value is that they enable us to feel present at the discussions of some very distinguished thinkers of another continent. We are presented not with the polished product of a piece of research but with ideas in the making, so that we can see what ideas are in the minds of these researchers and what aspects of problems are troubling them. Though privileged to be present, we are debarred from one thing—asking questions like the others. But with the growth of communication engineering this defect may disappear.

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

Alan Thompson. 'Cybernetics and Animal Integration.' The Irish Journal of Medical Science, July, 1951, pp. 297–303.

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom  

The main cybernetic ideas are briefly introduced. The author then gives examples from physiology and pathology that can be interpreted by means of feed-back mechanism. 'The control of the p H of the blood through variation in the depth of respiration, the regulation of the body temperature, and the adjustment of cardiac action and of blood pressure through the mediation of the carotid sinus, are self-regulating functions embodying negative feed-back, …' (p. 300). Disorders of feed-back systems are illustrated by Cheyne-Stokes breathing (negative feed-back); chronic hypertensive renal disease (positive feed-back); cerebellar incoordination; possibly also 'dysdiadokokinesia, clonus, and the spontaneous movements of Parkinsonian tremor and chorea' (p. 301). It is suggested that sleep may be a mechanism for the avoidance or correction of disorders by allowing useless electroneural reverberations to subside. The analogy is pointed out between treating a disordered machine by switching off the abnormal sub-system, disrupting permanently such a system, and giving the entire machine a shock on the one hand, and on the other treating a disordered mind by deep sedation, pre-frontal leucotomy, and E.C.T. A cautionary note is included that the whole subject is only in the stage of being a working hypothesis.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

D. G. MacRae. 'Cybernetics and Social Science.' The British Journal of Sociology, 2, 1951, pp. 135–49.

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom  

'Cybernetics is the analytic study of the isomorphisms of communication structure in mechanisms,

506

organisms, and societies' (p. 135). The author traces the development of the non-Newtonian concept of irreversible time-order; statistical physics, and communication engineering. The relevance of time is: 'Cybernetics is concerned with the principles that govern the maintenance of structure in time both within the organism and in its external relations' (p. 138). The relevance of statistics is, according to the author, that communication is temporal and can therefore be regarded as statistical. From this he proceeds to feed-backs, 'information', and analogies between machines and organisms.

After this preparation, the author raises his question of the relation between cybernetics and society. His view is given by: 'It is possible, for example, to re-state a good deal of post-Keynesian economics in terms of a kind of monetary Parkinsonianism. The trade cycle may be regarded as a mechanism with an excessively large feed-back, but I cannot see that there is any real advantage to be gained by the economist in this process, which partakes rather of the nature of a translation than of an analysis' (p. 145); 'I do not believe that a new science is about to develop, but I am convinced that Professor Wiener has established genuine isomorphisms between the brain and nervous structure of calculating and self-correcting automatic machinery'; 'I am very unconvinced that Professor Wiener has been able to establish helpful isomorphisms with society. … On the other hand, I have not the slightest doubt that attempting to think oneself into this new attitude with its new language, makes clearer the nature of a significant number of dynamic social processes' (p. 148). The author considers that cybernetics is not useful where short runs of statistical data are available: 'the small size of the population involved in even the largest human groups and the very large number of variants involved, make me certain that the language of institutional society is the correct form of discourse for dealing with the problem of communication (p. 146).

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

D. M. MacKay. 'Mindlike Behaviour in Artefacts.' The British Journal for the Philosophy of Science, 2, 1951, pp. 105–21.

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom  

Goal-directed activity of an artifact is described as minimizing the interval between its present state and its goal. All examples of this are characterized by 'circulation of information', i.e. by feed-back. A suitably designed artifact is capable of receiving, selecting, storing, and sending information, reacting to changes in its 'universe', reasoning deductively, controlling its own activity so as to further some goal, and changing its pattern of behaviour as a result of experience. These capacities are consequences of the principle that 'any pattern of observable behaviour which can be specified in terms of unique and precisely-definable reactions to precisely-definable situations can in principle be imitated mechanically' (p. 108). Further an artifact can be designed with a statistical device, so that its behaviour could not be predicted precisely but only with probability. By this means an artifact can display analogues to prejudices, preferences, originality, and capacity to learn. This is stated as a principle: 'Any pattern of observable behaviour which can be defined statistically, in terms of probable reactions to given situations, can in principle be shown by an artefact' (p. 112). Abstraction or recognition of pattern also has its analogue in artefacts. By reproducing patterns that resemble patterns of stimuli, a process here called 'replicating stimuli', an artifact has a means by which it can describe its own experience. It could even learn, it is stated, to recognize patterns it had not been built to recognize, and to form abstract hypotheses. In short the author fails 'to find any distinction in principle between the observable behaviour of a human brain and the behaviour possible in a suitably designed artefact' (p. 120). In addition he briefly considers the bearing of his results on the Cartesian concept of mind, dualism, identity of the self, and freedom of the will.

Soviet Psychiatry

By Joseph Wortis. (New York: The Williams and Wilkins Company, 1950. London: Baillière, Tindall and Cox, 1951. Pp. xvi + 314. 38 s. 6 d.)

M. Balint

M. Balint

M. Balint
London

M. Balint  

'Psychiatry, ' wrote Giliarovsky (one of the leading psychiatrists in the U.S.S.R.) some years ago, 'more than any field of medical science, reflects the social atmosphere in which it develops: it can be said that each epoch has its own kind of psychiatry.' A change in economic structure, he contended, affects the science of psychiatry, the methods of the psychiatrist, and the mind of the patient, each in distinctive ways. 'We can even see', said

63

Giliarovsky, 'in the very minds of our patients the reflections of profound social upheavals, the reflections of the class struggle in psychiatry. There has always been a general expectation among Soviet psychiatrists that something new and distinctive was bound to develop in their own science' (page 129)

This book is a conscientious and exhaustive report on these 'new and distinctive developments'. Dr. Wortis worked through the whole available Russian literature on psychiatry, and every one of his details is substantiated by an exact quotation. His book consequently can be considered as an authoritative source for the study of Russian psychiatry up to 1950. His approach, though not uncritical, is mainly sympathetic. Perhaps this is the reason why both he and his publisher had to write a kind of apology to preface the book.

The picture of Soviet psychiatry as it emerges from the reading of this book is puzzling indeed. On the one hand there are undeniable signal successes, such as the efficient dealing with the vast problem of criminality and delinquency after the devastations of the Civil War, the amazingly low figures of psychiatric morbidity, the elaborate and apparently competent service of rehabilitation of psychiatric patients, etc. On the other hand one is struck by the alarming superficiality of the case histories and the almost complete absence of psychotherapy in the Western sense, as revealed in this book.

One reason for this paradoxical situation is the present stage of Soviet theory about man's place in his world. 'Co-operative work for useful social ends (and not the force of sexual drive) is regarded as the basic force that links man to man' (page 176). It is above all man's relationship to his work that determines whether he is content, happy, and healthy. Consequently every psychotherapy must aim first and foremost at restoring the patient's ability to work and thus establishing his confidence as a useful member of the community. We should call such a therapy a super-ego education, and doubtless most of the psychotherapy described in this book belongs to this class. In contrast, psycho-analysis is first and foremost an ego education, and consequently individualistic and not necessarily social. It would be a highly interesting research project to examine whether this striking difference in psycho-therapeutical orientation is due to the fact that the present Western civilization can be considered as comparatively stable while the Russian has been changing at very great speed.

responseSet.title: Beethoven and his Nephew
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Beethoven and his Nephew11

Richard Sterba and Editha Sterba

Editha Sterba

Richard Sterba
Editha Sterba
DETROIT

Editha Sterba  

The vast and still growing literature about Beethoven deals not only with the musical creations of the great composer but to an amazing extent also with his personality. The fascinating impact of this unique, powerful, bizarre, and wilful man affects us to this very day. When Sigmund Freud remarks in Moses and Monotheism that great men move us to acknowledge them such by something beyond the admiration of their grandiose creations, he mentions three examples: Goethe, Leonardo da Vinci, and Beethoven.2 The psychologist faces no easy task in attempting to apply his knowledge to such a great figure. Hero worship and glorification have deposited layers of distortion even within himself, and he must remove these in order to recognize the emotional strivings and defences which motivate the great man just as they do ordinary human beings.

We know little of Beethoven's childhood. His father, who conducted his early musical education, was a very harsh and severe man who drank heavily. His mother was a quiet, somewhat disillusioned, even-tempered woman who seemingly did not try too hard to protect her son from his father's tyranny and ill-treatment. She died of consumption when Beethoven was seventeen. Those who knew the child Ludwig describe him as an introverted, brooding, and wilful boy who seemed deeply attached to his mother.

Beethoven had two brothers, Karl four years and Johann, six years his junior. Soon after his mother's death his father was dismissed from his post as a court musician at Bonn for disorderly conduct, and his pension was paid to his son Ludwig, who was also a court musician of the Elector. At the age of seventeen Beethoven thus became the head of the family and combined the roles of father and mother in relation to his younger siblings. Brother Karl was then thirteen and brother Johann eleven. Beethoven's biographers emphasize the heavy burden which he had to assume at this early age, but we learn at the same time that this period of responsibility for his family was also a rather happy one, in which the talented young musician made many important social contacts and received wide recognition as a pianist and composer. For further musical education the Elector sent him to Vienna to study with Joseph Haydn. During his leave of absence the French invaded the Electorate, so that Beethoven could not return. This, with his success in the capital, led him to settle permanently in Vienna. His performance as a pianist soon secured him a large enough income to continue his protective and supporting attitude towards his brothers. He urged them to come to Vienna, and they followed him there. He found work for Karl, the elder, who was a musician of sorts, while Johann, who had finished his apprenticeship as a pharmacist, found a job as an apothecary. In the meantime their father had died.

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

W. Grey Walter. 'An Electro-mechanical "Animal".' Discovery, 11, 1950, pp. 90–3.

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom  

This brief article describes an electro-mechanical 'tortoise', how it works, and the varieties of behaviour it can display. No doubt some readers have seen one of these tortoises wandering about at the Festival of Britain. It can wander in random fashion over the floor and when it meets an obstacle it sidles away. When its current runs down, it goes back to a 'hutch' where it automatically connects with charging current, and when recharged it disconnects itself and sets forth once more.

[[RunningHead]]

The Homosexual in America

By Donald Webster Cory. (Greenberg, New York. Price $4.00.)

S. Yates

S. Yates

S. Yates  

The Homosexual in America is really an anthropological study written by 'one of the natives': how the homosexual thinks and lives and what are his social customs. The book could be regarded from another angle, as an interesting study of the interaction of psychological and social pressures and how a vicious circle results; the homosexual's behaviour is exaggerated and, in turn, shocks society so that he is ostracized even more stringently and is driven to really anti-social conduct which in itself has little to do with homosexuality. One of the most interesting sections is Chapter XIII which tells of the difficulties of a permanent union between homosexuals.

The author puts up a very good case for the repeal of laws against homosexuality; he also shows that homosexuality in America has become one of the minority problems like the Jewish and the Negro questions. The author has studied psycho-analytic literature on homosexuality and refers to it frequently. With some psycho-analytic conclusions he agrees, but others he would like to modify.

This book, though somewhat overlong, is a sincere and courageous attempt to face up to the problems of homosexuality. It has a special message for homosexuals and its perusal might well give physicians and teachers an insight into a thorny question.

501

[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy

By Paul Schilder. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul Ltd., 1951. Pp. xi + 383. 30 s.)

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

This book was first published in 1938. The present enlarged and revised edition, arranged by Schilder's widow, Dr. Lauretta Bender, provides a definitive statement of his attitude towards and technique of psychotherapy. It forms a valuable introduction to psychotherapy for any student of psychiatry; the author himself says that he 'attempts to survey the field of psychotherapy in general' and has not tried to write an introduction to psycho-analysis.

In the original edition the introduction to the general principles of psychotherapy was based on a survey of the psychology of organic and psychopathological symptomatology and of somatic and psychic health as experiences. The 'technical outlines of psychotherapy' were dealt with by discussing the relationship of the physician to the patient as seen in the various psychotherapeutic systems and during group psychotherapy of Schilder's own unique type. The final third of the book dealt with treatment of the specific types of neuroses, psychoses, and psychopathies.

In the present edition the additions consist chiefly of relevant excerpts from papers published by Schilder since 1938. Additional material concerning the treatment and prevention of crime, mental health in relationship to childhood, adolescence, studenthood, old age, etc., and further information concerning the use of drugs, hypnosis, etc., during psychotherapy, and the implications of insulin treatment, shock treatment, etc., on psychotherapy are included.

All teachers who wish to ensure that their students know the eclecticism of Schilder's approach will greatly value this book. To those who have never heard Schilder speak, his manner of teaching is still pleasantly apparent in reading it. An appended bibliography of 22 pages forms a valuable reading list on problems related to psychotherapy.

Schilder's attitude to psycho-analytic treatment at the time he wrote this book is inadequately stated in the few pages devoted to describing psycho-analysis as a psychotherapeutic system. The student will have to read Schilder's other writings to build up a clearer picture of the way Schilder valued and criticized psycho-analysis. Nevertheless he ends his discussion of psycho-analysis by writing 'further development of psychotherapy which does not utilize Freud's work is unthinkable.

responseSet.title: Pregenital Patterning
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pregenital Patterning

Phyllis Greenacre M.D.

Phyllis Greenacre

Phyllis Greenacre, M.D.
NEW YORK

Phyllis Greenacre  

This paper is based on a more condensed presentation of the same material at the meeting of the International Psycho-Analytic Congress in Amsterdam this summer. At that meeting it was striking that the interest in the pregenital development was widespread. Indeed the greater number of papers at the Congress dealt with some aspects of pregenital developmental problems, and one heard occasionally among the unofficial comments some concern that the interest was veering away from the Oedipus complex. My own investigation offends in this respect in so far as it focuses chiefly on questions of pregenital patterning rather than on specific variations of the oedipal period and the fate of the Oedipus complex itself. That the oedipal period is the most momentous era of psychic and emotional organization and the Oedipus complex the most significant network of conflicts throughout the entire life has been amply verified throughout the years. The crossing of this boundary between infancy and childhood is more perilous even, though less dramatic in physical and direct behavioural changes, than the somewhat comparable epochal passing from childhood to young adulthood at puberty. It also has much greater and more lasting resonances throughout the entire life. The interest in the pregenital appears to arise from the generally increasing awareness that the experiences which have already been accumulated may be as important in the fate of the Oedipus complex as the special outer circumstances which are present in the oedipal period itself. It is often the special struggles of the oedipal period, re-enacted in puberty or in the early years of expected social maturity of the twenties, that precipitate the overt psychotic or neurotic break, and the relatively sudden eruption of disturbances which previously may have been more or less concealed though the symptoms themselves will ultimately unfold the hidden preoedipal history.

The description of the infant's organization on the basis of the sensorimotor growth and emotional relationships of the first five years of life in terms of a fairly regular succession of dominant drives was the work of the early years of analysis, and gave rise to the formulation of the libido theory which has been the backbone of analytic understanding, importantly supplemented in later years by study of other aspects of individuation, viz., the development of the ego. It was on the background of the understanding of the libidinal growth of the organism that the concepts of fixation, repression, and regression resulting from traumas, so important in the early work on neuroses, were developed and significant patterns of organization in different neuroses were differentiated. While there has been some displeasure with the libido theory in recent years, since not all symptomatic pictures would fit well into its constructions, still it seems to most of us that the descriptions of the libidinal phases, achieved first wholly through clinical phenomenological studies, has been well supported by later studies of biological maturation of the infant, a basic point of view now used by many pediatricians and psychologists observing children.

[[RunningHead]]

Christians and Jews: A Psychoanalytic Study

By Rudolph M. Loewenstein. (New York: International Universities Press, 1951. Pp. 224. 26 s.)

Jacob Arlow M.D.

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow  

Too much cannot be written on the subject of anti-Semitism. What in the nineteenth century was a blot on nations became in the middle of the twentieth century an international scourge. To the scientific and hypothetical objective observer the reasons advanced for hating the Jews must seem fantastic, contradictory, and incomprehensible, a challenge to human credulity. It is not surprising, therefore, that an ever-growing number of psycho-analysts have directed their very special investigative techniques towards this perplexing psychological problem. Of the various books which have appeared on the subject, the present volume is unquestionably the best.

What distinguishes this book is the precise formulation of the dynamics of anti-Semitism as a phenomenon of individual and group psychology.

64

responseSet.title: Further Remarks about Schreber's Hallucinations
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Further Remarks about Schreber's Hallucinations11

M. Katan M.D.

M. Katan

M. Katan, M.D.
PSYCHIATRIC DEPARTMENT, WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
CLEVELAND, OHIO

M. Katan  

This paper is a sequel to one which I read two years ago at the Congress in Zürich (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 31, 1950). Let me state some of the conclusions reached in that first paper before commencing my present one.

Schreber, in the period preceding his psychotic delusional state, tried to ward off the demands of his unconscious homosexual urge. Yet one night his defences broke down and he experienced an outburst of six emissions. The castration danger involved in these orgasms caused him to sever his relations with reality, whereupon his psychosis began.

It should not be assumed that Schreber's contact with reality was now completely broken. A large part of his personality still remained in contact with reality. Only if a danger arises with which the ego cannot cope by reality means is contact with reality relinquished and a psychotic symptom formed instead.

During the early stage of Schreber's psychosis, every time that the non -psychotic part of his personality anticipated a situation in which his homosexual feelings would gain the upper hand and would lead to an orgasm, his ego did not wait until such a situation had fully developed, but set up an interference. With regard to the complicated process which then followed and which I have described in my previous paper, I wish to emphasize only that the cathexis of the dangerous homosexual urge is withdrawn and that this withdrawn energy is used in forming the hallucination. In this psychotic process a very primitive form of projection is employed. What was originally part of the id appears now, in a distorted form, to come from the outside.

We may say that the energy which was withdrawn from the dangerous urge evaporates in forming the hallucination. The hallucination, therefore, is a discharge phenomenon! The amount of energy which would lead to the development of danger is discharged, and the conflict is temporarily resolved. New stimuli—either from within or from without—will again cause an increase of the homosexual urge, and the whole process is repeated.

responseSet.title: Oral Components of the Castration Complex
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Oral Components of the Castration Complex11

René De Monchy M.D.

René De Monchy

René De Monchy, M.D.
STOCKHOLM

René De Monchy  

The topic of this paper is not a new one. In Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety, for example, Freud wrote: 'Castration can be pictured on the basis of the daily experience of the faeces being separated from the body or on the basis of losing the mother's breast at weaning'. Several other authors have made contributions to the subject.2 The first, and in my opinion still the best, is Dr. Stärcke's paper published in 1921. Stärcke went much further than Freud and assumed that the emotional weight of the castration complex is mainly derived from oral frustration.

The reason I take up the subject again is its great practical importance, which is perhaps not always sufficiently appreciated. Even Freud, the originator of the concept of the close relationship between genital and pregenital instincts, might have laid still more stress on the therapeutic value of his discovery. In Analysis Terminable and Interminable, he wrote: 'We often feel that when we have reached the wish for a penis and the masculine protest, we have penetrated all the psychological strata and reached bedrock and that our task is accomplished', and in a footnote: 'What they (men) reject is not passivity in general but passivity in relation to men. That is to say, the masculine protest is in fact nothing other than fear of castration'. The expression 'our task is accomplished' suggests that castration anxiety and penis envy must be accepted as ultimate facts, which should be independent of earlier experiences. Freud must have meant those primary reactions in living beings that can so easily and in their purest form be demonstrated in animals. Von Uexküll and Lorenz call them angeborene Reaktionsschema, congenital reaction schemes. Animal psychology offers many examples of such reactions.

I will give two examples: Woodgrouse react at the sight of a sparrow-hawk in the air in typical fashion: the cock takes up a defensive position, the hen huddles up and seeks shelter. Young birds that have been kept isolated from birth react in the same way if a piece of paper of approximately the form of a hawk is moved over the cage. When the birds are twenty days old the aforementioned difference in behaviour between the cocks and the hens can already be noted. When the piece of paper, the attrappe, as the animal psychologist calls it, is made more and more unlike the hawk, the reaction grows progressively weaker and finally disappears.

News, Notes and Comments

18 th International Psycho-Analytical Congress, 1953. The British Psycho-Analytical Society, acting on the authorization of the 17th (Amsterdam) Congress, has proposed to the Executive of the Int.PA.A. that the 18th Congress be held at Bedford College (Regent's Park), London, commencing on Sunday, July 26, 1953.

A Conference of the European Branches of the International Psycho-Analytic Association. The British Psycho-Analytical Society plans to hold a conference in July 1952 and has invited members, associates and students of the European Branch Societies of the Int.PA.A. to participate.

The purpose of the Conference will be to enable scientific discussions to take place in small panels around a series of topics submitted by the participants. Apart from social events the conference will assemble in full only for the opening and the final sessions. The date of the conference has provisionally been fixed from Friday, July 4, 3 p.m., to Sunday, July 6, noon. All meetings will take place at the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 63 New Cavendish Street, London, W.1. Members from Societies overseas will be welcome and should communicate with the Secretary, Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 63 New Cavendish Street, London, W.1.

Clarence P. Oberndorf. A dinner in honour of Dr. Clarence P. Oberndorf, distinguished psychiatrist and psycho-analyst, on the occasion of his seventieth birthday, will be given by his friends and colleagues on Friday evening, February 15, 1952, at the Waldorf Astoria in New York City. The British Society take this opportunity of offering Dr. Oberndorf their felicitations and of expressing their thanks for his invaluable help in the conduct of this journal.

Dr. Marjorie Brierley, a former member of the Editorial Board of the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, has been appointed Assistant Editor in succession to the late Dr. John Rickman. Dr. Brierley is well known to the readers of this journal by her various papers on the theory and practice of psycho-analysis. These were published last year in her book Trends in Psycho-Analysis (reviewed by J. C. Flugel in Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 32, bd 259–261).

responseSet.title: Technical Problems in Analysis of Psychosomatic Disorders with Special Reference to Precision in Short-Term Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Technical Problems in Analysis of Psychosomatic Disorders with Special Reference to Precision in Short-Term Psychotherapy11

Flanders Dunbar M.D.

Flanders Dunbar

Flanders Dunbar, M.D.
NEW YORK

Flanders Dunbar  

SUMMARY

In this paper the purpose has been:

(1) To call attention to the need of improving precision in short-term psychotherapy, not to suggest that such therapy is suitable for all patients.

(2) To suggest a means of selecting from among a large group of persons in need of treatment, for whom psycho-analysis is impossible or undesirable, those likely to respond best to briefer methods.

(3) To point out that, in determining the treatment of choice, the psychic and somatic components in the illness syndrome must be evaluated not only quantitively but also qualitatively. The nature of the character neurosis has a bearing on physiological dysfunction; for example a compulsive character suffering from asthma needs a therapeutic regimen which is very different from that needed by a compulsive character suffering from hypertension.

(4) To illustrate the peculiar hazards inevitably encountered in short-term treatment and the reasons why some analysts hesitate to accept the responsibilities involved. Suggestions are made as to some adjustments in technique which may help in coping with the difficulties inherent in the method. These adjustments are particularly needed in the timing of appointments, the giving of interpretations, and the management of transference.

(5) To point out the service that skilled psycho-analysts may render if they are willing to direct their attention to this somewhat neglected field of research and therapy.

In conclusion it is urged that psychiatrists who undertake short-term treatment do so only on a basis of thorough training in psycho-analysis and considerable experience in its practice. Whatever his brilliance as technician, the psycho-analyst, if he is to heal, remains first of all a physician.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Essays in Applied Psycho-Analysis

By Ernest Jones. (London: The Hogarth Press and Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 1951. Two volumes. International Psycho-Analytical Library, Nos. 40 and 41. Pp. 333 and 381. 21 s. each.)

J. Flugel

J. Flugel

J. Flugel  

These essays constitute a second and much enlarged edition of the book which appeared under the same title in 1923 and which was reviewed at some length in this Journal, 1924, 5, p. 373. One essay which appeared in the first edition (that on Hamlet) is omitted from the present one, having appeared in book form under the title Hamlet and Oedipus. The remaining twelve are included, with a few additional notes, though divided among the two volumes, the first of which bears the sub-title 'Miscellaneous Essays', while the second is devoted to 'Essays in Folklore and Religion'. There are in all twenty-four new essays dealing with a wide variety of subjects, some of which have not appeared in print before, while others have been published in a number of books and journals (seven of them in this Journal) in this and other countries. Their collection in the present new edition is an event of importance which undoubtedly provides a very valuable addition to the literature of applied psycho-analysis, for though these essays vary considerably in length and profundity of treatment, every one of them displays in greater or less degree the erudition and psychological penetration which we have long since learnt to expect from all Ernest Jones's writings.

The twenty-four new essays could be classified in various ways, but perhaps the most convenient division is into two main groups, those devoted to more or less specific problems, and those dealing with the more general problems of the relation of psycho-analysis to other approaches or other fields of study. As it would be impossible in a review of ordinary length to present anything resembling a full account of the rich content of these new essays, and as some of them must already be known to readers of this Journal, it will perhaps be most helpful to enumerate the titles of those falling under these two heads. In the first group we find fourteen essays devoted respectively to 'The Inferiority Complex of the Welsh', 'The Problem of Paul Morphy', 'The Psychology of Constitional Monarchy', 'Can Civilization be Saved?', 'Evolution and Revolution', 'The Psychology of Quislingism', 'The Psychology of the Jewish Question', 'Psychopathology and International Tension', 'The Death of Hamlet's Father', 'Beliefs concerning the Nightmare', 'Mother Right and the Sexual Ignorance of Savages', 'A Psycho-Analytical Note on Palæolithic Art', 'Free Will and Determinism', 'The Significance of Christmas'.

responseSet.title: Examination Anxiety without Examination
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Examination Anxiety without Examination

H. Thorner

H. Thorner

H. A. Thorner
LONDON

H. Thorner  

1. Introduction

Examination anxiety has mainly been investigated in patients who had difficulties in examinations. These difficulties may be excessive anxiety, inability to study, or repeated failure in examinations. But the same patients also complain of similar anxiety in situations that are far removed from examination proper. For example, Sadger's patient, in discussing a dream, gave the following description of his sexual difficulties: 'Each attempt at sexual intercourse appears to me as an examination … in my imagination a fear of the new examination remains: this means that every fresh attempt at intercourse is an examination for the unconscious and I am unable to solve this problem. Therefore I must try to deceive the woman by feigning passion which I do not feel. In the dream, I do not succeed in the deception.'1

In the following I shall describe an anxiety reaction which I found in a number of patients who did not complain of examination anxiety. One patient came to analysis for impotence, others for depression, or phobic reactions. But the analysis revealed that the patients had impotence, or frigidity, in common and that they showed a marked readiness to react with anxiety resembling examination anxiety.

Although these patients had not been aware of the form of their anxiety reaction, they were ready to acknowledge it after they had experienced it in the transference situation. It is one of my points that I do not deal with symptoms of which the patient had originally been aware, but of signs which became manifest in the analytical situation and could be analysed there, and then acknowledged by the patient as a reaction also occurring outside the consulting room. But I hope that this discussion may also throw light on the nature and psychopathology of examination anxiety proper.

2. Examination Anxiety and Castration Fear

The analytic work on the subject has been summarized by Flugel, who, however, omitted Sadger's paper.2 Flugel, following Rank and others, dealt with examination from the historical and anthropological point of view as an initiation rite. From this point of view, which is mainly that of the examiner, the examinee appears as someone who, willingly or unwillingly, has to submit to the procedure of examination.
