responseSet.title: The Unconscious Phantasy of an Inner World Reflected in Examples from English Literature
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Unconscious Phantasy of an Inner World Reflected in Examples from English Literature

Joan Riviere

Joan Riviere

Joan Riviere
LONDON

Joan Riviere  

The inner world which in our unconscious phantasy each of us contains inside ourselves is one of those psycho-analytical concepts that most people find especially difficult to accept or understand. It is a world of figures formed on the pattern of the persons we first loved and hated in life, who also represent aspects of ourselves. The existence even in unconscious phantasy of these inner figures and of their apparently independent activities within us (which can be as real, or more real and actual, to us in unconscious feeling than external events) may seem incredible and incomprehensible; it might therefore perhaps be useful to approach the problem from the opposite end, as it were, that is from the conscious level. My aim in this contribution is essentially to forge a link between certain conscious experiences, which will be familiar to most people, and the proposition that phantasies of our containing other persons inside ourselves, though deeply unconscious, do exist. For this purpose I have selected some relevant passages from literature. Before discussing these, however, I will consider shortly the question why this proposition of internal objects seems so difficult to accept.

It was of course Freud who first recognized the existence of the 'introjected object' as a regular phenomenon, a normal part of the personality, namely, in his formulation of the mental institution he called the super-ego, mainly based on the person of the father and represented consciously in our minds by what we call the conscience in each of us. Melanie Klein, however, in her explorations of unconscious phantasy through her work with very young children, has pursued this theme and brought to light much more concerning the persons in the inner world whom each of us individually has felt or feels to be part of himself. There is a difference between Freud's super-ego, a single differentiated function of our mental make-up, modelled though it may be on the personalities of parents, and the 'personal relations', however primitive and fantastic, we have had with the figures who people our inner worlds. When Freud published his Ego and Id his concept of the internalized parent as the super-ego did not rouse much resistance; it is true that it had already been introduced in a more acceptable form under the title of ego-ideal. Nevertheless, before very long an emotional reaction to the concept of the super-ego was manifested in a new view of analytic therapy; a move for the 'dissolution of the super-ego' was even initiated by Alexander at the Salzburg Congress, 1924, and found considerable support at the time. (This view is to be distinguished from the general recognition that the curative effects of analysis are in part attributable to a reduction of the severity of the super-ego.) The point I wish to recall here is the emotional enthusiasm greeting Alexander's idea which virtually stigmatized the independent internal object in the self as something morbid. In the enthusiasm for this view, which Freud himself did what he could to discourage, we had, I think, the first flicker of the suspicion and intolerance often enough manifested against the concept of internal objects.

responseSet.title: Metapsychological Considerations on the Concept of Work
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Metapsychological Considerations on the Concept of Work55

Barbara Lantos

Barbara Lantos

Barbara Lantos
LONDON

Barbara Lantos  

In an earlier paper, called 'On Work and the Instincts', 1 I expressed the observation that although in our analytical practice disturbances of working capacity come next in importance to disturbances of sexuality—the question of what work is, is not treated as a problem. We look upon work as an obvious necessity, the mastering of which is a proof of good adaptation to reality, whereas its neurotic disturbances indicate failure in this adaptation.

I was impressed to find the same considerations in Hendrick's paper on 'Work and the Pleasure Principle'2 where he says: 'Work itself remains a challenging problem. Though working capacity is obviously a most important result of normal ego-organization, its essential contribution to biological survival, its utility to the individual and to society have been taken more or less for granted when no pathology is involved. Work has naturally been accepted by analysts as an essential function which is automatically restored when a neurosis is successfully treated rather than being itself the subject for intensive psychological analysis'.

In a recent paper on the 'Psychopathology of Work'3 Oberndorf gives some metapsychological considerations to the concepts of work and play and their interdependence. His paper is concerned, however, with clinical aspects. It was gratifying to find that his theoretical considerations are running on lines similar to those which were expressed in my previous paper.

Hendrick, in his paper, formulates the problem in metapsychological terms, when asking: 'What instinct provides the energy and the need to exercise the physiological organs available for work and hence leads to the development of the integrated functions of the ego? We have assumed—says Hendrick—that all executant functions at any age are responses to an instinct differing in its goal, purpose, and pleasure-mechanism from the libido and have chosen to call this the " instinct to master " or "mastery-instinct."'

What Hendrick, in his exposition, calls the partial ego-functions, and executant functions of the ego, covers, to my mind, those parts of the mental apparatus which Hartmann called the autonomous ego—and with this part of his assumptions one certainly agrees; but one is somehow concerned about the disruption of the Instinct Theory, when one finds a new basic instinct added to the interplay between Eros and aggression. The existence of an instinct to master, as a component instinct, is postulated by Freud in his Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality, and its far-reaching implications should not be denied; its elevation by Hendrick to a basic instinct, of the same order as libido and aggression, is probably due to the attempt to explain structural phenomena in dynamic terms.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Childhood and Society

By Erik H. Erikson. Imago Publishing Co. (No date.) Pp. 397. (Price 25 s.)

S. Foulkes

S. Foulkes

S. Foulkes  

This book, while making an important contribution

497

from psycho-analysis to social science, is at the same time a most welcome enrichment of psycho-analytic thought. The author draws on his work as a psycho-analyst, and especially as a child analyst, as well as on his experience in anthropological field work in this study of the warp and woof of childhood and society. Case material of children, American Indians and war casualties is illumined by psycho-analytic understanding of neurotic conflict.

Erikson demonstrates both the familiar and its reverse: that the individual's behaviour as a member of society is determined by childhood experiences, but also that these very experiences depend in their turn on the milieu in which they happen. They vary from culture to culture, from class to class. Certain events will only occur in certain cultural settings; a particular stress will only obtain in a particular environment. Such events and stresses are handled and solved in no arbitrary manner, but in the strict tradition of the community concerned.

In complete contrast to the views held in many psycho-analytic circles (in their most extreme form by the British child analysts in their emphasis on the child's early phantasy life), Erikson underlines the societal factors through which, from earliest childhood, we are each one of us trained to fit into our communal surroundings. It is not planned or scientific training, but instinctive; growing into the particular pattern prevalent in each community only through continuous trial and error, and through constant repetition of the bitter consequences of each false step. The pattern is imposed by forces which are part of and are diffused through the whole network of the communal body. Parents and teachers are therefore not consciously aware that the way in which they bring up their children is absolutely dependent on the dictatorial direction of communal forces.

The first part of the book is concerned with childhood and the modalities of social life, and contains a most important restatement of the whole theory of infantile sexuality.

[[RunningHead]]

Mahatma Gandhi a Contribution to the Psycho-Analytic Understanding of the Causes of War and the Means of Preventing Wars11

F. Lowtzky Ph.D.

F. Lowtzky

F. Lowtzky, Ph.D.
JERUSALEM

F. Lowtzky  

In Memory of Dr. Max Eitingon

Politics without Religion is a mantrap, because it kills the soul—(Gandhi)

Because of the short time at my disposal, I can discuss only two features of the material contained in a more extensive study of the subject.

1. The achievement of India's independence.

2. The attainment of equal status for the Pariah Caste.

In his own country, Gandhi is revered as a saint, the rest of the world does not even comprehend him. How was it possible that a man without the powers of the state at his disposal, and without any coercive means, became the accepted ruler of one fifth of the world's population? How was it possible that Gandhi, who is rightly called 'The Father of India's Independence', achieved this independence by pacific means at a time when terror reigned in India and her people were full of hatred and revenge against Great Britain? Moreover, he obtained Caste equality without bloodshed. To throw light onto these problems and to understand their psycho-analytic meaning is the purpose of this paper.

Before he could realize the final task he had set himself, that is, the nationalization of property by these same peaceful means, he was murdered by the secretary of the extreme Nationalists, on 30th January, 1948.

Hatred of Great Britain was rampant in India during Gandhi's youth. Hundreds of years of oppression had completely altered India's character, and turned a peaceloving, friendly people into an aggressive nation, ready to wage war to achieve their liberation. Gandhi, as a boy of 15, was inspired by the desire to go to war against Great Britain. His people, he believed, were weak because they did not eat meat, and the English derived the strength which enabled them to master India from meat eating. In his childhood, he was afraid of the dark. He imagined ghosts coming from one side, thieves from another, and serpents from a third. Eating meat, he and a friend with whom he discussed it imagined, would cure all fear. They therefore decided that they too would eat meat, secretly. Gandhi's first attempt to eat goat's meat sickened him, and all that night he was haunted by a nightmare of a live goat bleating inside him. Furthermore, although he tried to steel himself with the reminder that meat eating was a duty to India—a 'reform'—his conscience gave him no peace, and he abandoned meat eating. At about the same time, Gandhi fell into the habit of smoking cigarettes, scavenging stumps, and pilfering the servants' coppers to buy cigarettes. This led him to the more serious theft of a link from his brother's gold armlet. His guilt was more than the 15-year-old Gandhi could bear, and he resolved to confess his sins and seek adequate punishment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Politics. A Contribution to the Psychology of Politics and Morals

By R. E. Money-Kyrle. (London: Gerald Duckworth, 1951. Pp. 183. 9 s.

J. Flugel

J. Flugel

J. Flugel  

Within the small compass of this volume there is contained much wisdom—or if that be too strong a word, at the least much food for profitable thought. Dr. Money-Kyrle was among the first (though not quite the first) to endeavour to make more or less generalized applications of psycho-analytic findings to ethical and social problems. In recent years there has appeared quite a number of volumes devoted to this subject. In the present book, however, he still maintains a kind of priority, inasmuch as he differs from the previous authors in making his approach largely from the point of view of Kleinian psychology. The evaluation of his work will thus inevitably to some extent be affected by the reader's estimate of the contributions made by Mrs. Klein and those who follow her. But whatever be the judgement on certain specific points there can be no doubt that in making the attempt he has forged another helpful link between what were formerly the separate disciplines of child psychology and social psychology—the kind of link in making which Freud himself excelled and to which is due so much of the wide illumination cast by psycho-analysis on human problems generally.

But at least two other factors have contributed largely to the author's viewpoint: his early philosophical training and his experience as a member of the German Personnel Research Branch (the 'spiritual creation' of Dr. H. V. Dicks) which did valuable work both in social survey and in personnel selection in the British Zone of Germany after the war. In virtue of the former, Money-Kyrle has become dissatisfied with the stand of ethical relativism which is so frequently adopted (often, as he freely admits, as a valuable safeguard against bias) in social science; while the latter drove home to him the lesson, already learnt from psycho-analysis, of the existence of a correspondence between upbringing and character, in the sense of a positive correlation between stern discipline in youth and an attitude of authoritarian obedience in later life, while a home training in which sympathy and reason play a greater part is connected with a more liberal and 'realistic' attitude based on a more tolerant super-ego of the kind which tends to be produced by successful analytic treatment. The author considers that on the basis of psycho-analytic knowledge it is possible to make certain at least provisional statements about the good or 'normal' mind, as one in which no aspect (e.g. of love, fear, guilt or hate) is permanently excluded from consciousness, and which in consequence is tolerant alike of physical, social, and psychological realities. A good state or nation is in turn one which fosters such a character in its citizens. Perhaps the first part of the book, concerned with philosophical reflections and with the early development of mind, may prove rather over-long for readers anxious to get on to 'political' implications, but the author obviously felt that these latter could only be properly appreciated against an adequate background of philosophical and psycho-analytical thought, and at any rate when he does start on his main theme in the second part of the book, almost every page contains some useful information or suggestion of a kind which is often foreign to the ordinary political thinker and which the latter would do well to ponder.

[[RunningHead]]

International Congress, 1953

The 18 th Congress of the International Psycho-Analytical Association will be held in London, at Bedford College, Regent's Park, London, N.W.1, from Sunday, July 26 to Thursday, July 30 (inclusive) 1953.

ANNOUNCEMENT OF PRELIMINARY PROGRAMME

Sunday, July 26. 10 a.m. to 5 p.m.: Registration for the Congress at Bedford College. Evening: Reception by the British Psycho-Analytical Society. Monday, July 27. Morning: Opening Address—General Session. Afternoon: Simultaneous Sessions—papers of general interest, each session composed of four papers grouped according to topic. Tuesday, July 28. Symposium on Schizophrenia. Morning: General Session—The Psychopathology (theory) of Schizophrenia. Afternoon: General Session—The Treatment of Schizophrenia. Wednesday, July 29. Morning: Simultaneous Sessions—each composed of four papers grouped according to topic. Afternoon: Two Panels (meeting simultaneously)—(1) Mechanisms of Defence and their place in Psycho-Analytic Technique. (2) Comparison of Problems of Training and of Therapeutic Analyses. (Open only to members.) Thursday, July 30. Morning: Business Meeting. Afternoon: Recreational Meeting.

Information of the Official Reception, the Congress Dinner and Excursions will be given later.

Titles and two copies of the Abstracts of individual papers should be sent to the Chairman of the Programme Committee before February 15, 1953: Dr. Phyllis Greenacre, 970 Park Avenue, New York City 28.

Membership of the Congress: Attention is drawn to the fact that membership of the Congress is only open to the following groups of people: (a) all those whose names were included in the list of members of the International Psycho-Analytical Association published in the INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, 1952, Vol. 33 (3). (b) Anyone not on this list whose application form has been countersigned by the Secretary of his local Psycho-Analytical Society. (c) Husbands or wives of members of the Congress.

Further details, including registration forms, will be circulated to all members of the International Psycho-Analytical Association during October 1952.

[[RunningHead]]

Failures in Psychiatric Treatment

Edited by Paul H. Hoch. (New York: Grune and Stratton, 1948. Pp. 241. 27 s.

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

This book consists of fifteen papers on failures in different fields of psychiatric treatment presented at a symposium held at the 37th Annual Meeting of the American Psychopathological Association in 1947.

Failures in psycho-analytic treatment are described by Dr. C. P. Oberndorf (pp. 10–20). He discusses the effects of unfavourable external conditions, the difficulties in applying psycho-analytic technique usually due to counter-transference problems, the effect of initial symptoms masking a psychiatric condition for which psycho-analytic treatment is not usually considered suitable, and finally the occasional patients whose personalities and symptoms justify psycho-analytic treatment which nevertheless is ineffective. He ends by making an appeal for research by psycho-analysts into the causes of failures and repeats the plea he made in 1942 (Amer. J. Psychiatry, 99, 374) that psycho-analysts make use of consultations with colleagues more frequently than is usual after, for instance, a period of unsuccessful treatment of 300 hours. S. Rado, in discussion, regretted that psycho-analysts had not yet developed definite criteria and prolonged follow-up in order to allow adequate statistical analysis of results.

[[RunningHead]]

Maternal Care and Mental Health

By J. Bowlby. World Health Organization: Monograph Series. (Geneva, 1951. Pp. 179. 10 s., $2.00. Obtainable U.K. from H.M. Stationery Office; U.S.A. from Columbia University Press.)

W. Gillespie

W. Gillespie

W. Gillespie  

Dr. Bowlby's book is the outcome of a temporary appointment with the World Health Organization in 1950. The result is a most impressive document which has one pervasive thesis, succinctly stated by the author: 'What is essential for mental health is that the infant and young child should experience a warm, intimate and continuous relationship with his mother (or mother substitute) in which both find satisfaction and enjoyment.' Maternal deprivation means the lack of this, from whatever cause. Partial deprivation leads to neurosis and instability of character. Complete deprivation may cripple entirely the capacity to make relationships.

Three types of evidence are reviewed—those based on direct observation, as in the work of Burlingham and Freud, Spitz and many others; retrospective studies such as those of Bowlby himself, Goldfarb and others; and follow-up studies, where Goldfarb's work is outstanding. The unanimity of so many investigators is certainly remarkable. Only three dissentients have been discovered by the author (Orgel, Brown, and Bodman), and he finds it possible to discount their findings on grounds of scientific method.

The evidence leads to the interim conclusion that prolonged maternal deprivation in the young child may have grave and far-reaching effects. Our knowledge of detail is small, but it seems likely that qualitative differences in the experiences of deprivation are connected with differences in outcome. Stealing, for instance, may depend on a break in love relationship and fail to appear if there has never been such a relationship. Deprivation in the second six months is universally agreed to be of great significance; many believe that this is true also of the first half-year, especially from three to six months.

Discussing theory, Bowlby draws an analogy from embryology in suggesting that the mother acts as a psychic 'organizer' whose presence is necessary at certain critical periods of development, particularly for successful identification. He gives much weight also to Goldfarb's finding that the capacity for abstract thinking, so essential for ego and super-ego development, is impaired in deprived children; and he attempts to outline theoretically the consequences of maternal deprivation occurring at various developmental stages. He does not minimize the difficulties of research, but gives some indications of how they may be met. The difficulty is in part one of satisfactory statistical treatment of the data; and Bowlby has given a welcome lead here by stating the degree of statistical significance attaching to the data he quotes.

[[RunningHead]]

Group Life

By Marshall C. Greco. (New York: Philosophical Library, 1950. $4.75.)

M. Balint

M. Balint

M. Balint
London

M. Balint  

Man ought to be considered not as an independent individual but as an indivisible and inseparable part of his actual group. Individuals do not exist as individuals, but only as members of social groups. To speak of their instincts or their individual history is incorrect and misleading; human needs, activities, pains and pleasures are always social, always involve other human beings. To show the author's ideas I quote some of his sentences: 'The neurotic symptom is not a subterfuge aimed at satiating a need that stands apart from the needs of the group; rather, it is a means for reconciling in the most effective manner the conflicting elements of the indivisible of which the neurotic is a part'. 'Freud conceptualized a theory of the neurosis that considered a complex, insulated, unconscious force as being the cause of the trouble.' 'Our own approach treats the single being, the neurotic, as a mere particle of the real whole. In this sense the neurotic is suffering the discords inherent in a panorama of group forces. Thus from orthodox psycho-analysis to the group-life theory each step was the result of covering life in terms of a larger whole.

63

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

Reid, John R. and Finesinger, Jacob E. 'The Role of Insight in Psychotherapy.' Amer. J. of Psychiatry, 108, No. 10, April, 1952, pp. 726–734.

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

This is a valuable discussion of the therapeutic process in psychotherapy (including psycho-analytic therapy) in which an attempt is made to clarify the meaning of insight. The authors conclude: 'With increased concern, on the part of serious workers, to record full interview materials, with the logical analysis of such materials and the consequent formulation of testable hypotheses about the relative efficacy of different forms of psychotherapy, with more critical questioning of assumptions and methods and less dogmatic theorizing about issues naively interpreted in an all-or-none fashion, with such advances in data gathering, logical thinking, and hypothesis testing, we may reasonably look forward to a time, in the not too distant future, when we can really answer the question, what is the rôle of insight in psychotherapy?

506

[[RunningHead]]

Inside out

Anton Ehrenzweig

Anton Ehrenzweig

Anton Ehrenzweig  

By Adrian Stokes. (Faber and Faber, 1947.)

Smooth and Rough. By Adrian Stokes. (Faber and Faber, 1951.)

It is fortunate that an art critic and historian of Adrian Stokes's standing should have become associated with the psycho-analytical movement. He freely acknowledges the debt which his æsthetic insight owes to his personal experience of psycho-analysis. His recent psychological conceptions of artistic creation are already foreshadowed in an earlier book Colour and Form (Faber, second edition, 1952) which has found a great deal of attention among artists. There Stokes expounds the integrating function of colour which links together disconnected object forms. This tension between disintegrating and re-integrating factors is now interpreted in depth-psychological terms. Inside Out deals with the externalization of the artist's internal conflict into the work of art. In merely looking at nature we may project our tendencies towards destruction or reparation into the land-scape. Stokes recalls autobiographically how to him as a small child the park in which he had to play seemed polluted, full of sinister secrets or threatening destruction, and how he felt a desperate wish to 'put it right'. The park scene might have symbolically represented the torn body of the mother destroyed in unconscious aggressive phantasies. In the desolation which the artist might feel in looking at reality the same unconscious phantasies might be at work. But the creative mind has the power to 'put it right'. The quarry which yields the builder's raw material appears as an ugly wound; the architect, however, fits the broken stones again into the organism of the house, representing the inviolate body of the mother, 'as if the smooth body of the wall-face, or the smooth vacancy within the apertures, were the shining breast, while the mouldings, the projections, the rustications, the tiles, were the head, the feeding nipple of that breast' (Smooth and Rough, p. 61).

Stokes anticipated or at least paralleled the findings of H. B. Lee and H. Segal (see her A Psycho-Analytical Approach to Aesthetics, this JOURNAL, 33, 2). Common to all these writers is an insight that the primary phase of artistic activity was destructive or even self-destructive, calling up the integrating function of creative activity. (My own analysis of artistic perception techniques also points to a primary disintegration of 'surface perception' to be made good in the æsthetic experience.) Compared with such theoretical formulations striving towards precision and compactness, it may appear somewhat difficult to follow Adrian Stokes into his day-dreams of sadness and hope.

[[RunningHead]]

Spontaneous Fluctuations in Depth of Hypnosis and their Implications for Ego-Function11

Margaret Brenman Ph.D. , Merton Gill M.D. and Robert Knight M.D.

Robert Knight

Robert Knight  

Early in our research, when we were investigating the possible uses of hypnosis as a 'shortcut' in psycho-analysis, we came upon a curious phenomenon, a phenomenon to which at first we paid little attention, but which gradually became one of the focal points in our inquiry into the nature of hypnosis. Anyone who has worked with hypnosis, particularly in a therapeutic setting, will probably recognize as a familiar occurrence the kind of event to which we refer: In the middle of a discussion, the patient will suddenly report a spontaneous change in his subjective experience. He may say, 'I'm going much deeper into hypnosis now', or he may say, 'I'm getting lighter; I'm coming out'.

In our zeal to inquire into the effects of introducing the hypnotic state into the ordinary course of a psycho-analysis, we at first regarded such spontaneous reports as a help if the report were 'I'm going deeper', or as a technical obstacle if it were 'I'm coming up lighter'. Often, if the latter were reported we would respond by redoubling our effort to deepen the hypnosis in order to 'get on with the business in hand'. After all, if one were to study hypnosis as an auxiliary in psycho-analysis, one had to have the patient in a hypnotic state.

It was only when our research interests began to depart somewhat from the practical-therapeutic and to become more theoretical that the phenomenon, per se, began to excite our curiosity enough to warrant a period of intensive study.

Before describing our procedure, we should like to say a few words about the methodological problems inherent in clinical research of this kind. The clinical researcher is often in the position of the geologist or the astronomer who comes upon a natural phenomenon, has a 'hunch' about its meaning, but cannot proceed to check his 'hunch' by direct experimentation. What then is the clinical investigator to do? He may content himself with his plausible hypothesis and extend his speculations within a self-contained theoretical system, hoping that future observations will confirm his hypothesis. This can be a highly valid activity, midway between literature and science, and in the hands of intuitive, yet disciplined, investigators it probably accounts for the bulk of our present knowledge about the human psyche. The most obvious disadvantage of this approach is that the prime test of the 'rightness' of the hypothesis, barring the test of time, is the investigator's own certainty that his hunch 'makes sense' or fits in neatly with things he has thought before. If the clinical investigator is temperamentally more faithful to the demands of science, he will make vigorous efforts to escape such solipsism in several ways: he will demand of himself that he observe and report as factually as possible; he will relentlessly try to make some distinction between the phenomena outside of himself and his own thought-patterns, i.e. an awareness of the line between observation and theory; he will hold as one criterion of the 'plausibility' of his hypothesis that it fit all known facts regarding the phenomenon in question; and finally he will be on the lookout for ways and means to validate his hunches. While the experimental researcher regards all these propositions as truisms, the clinical researcher must constantly remind himself of them and often fall far short of meeting them. Because our science is still so young we must often be content with the statement of the 'plausible hypothesis'.

responseSet.title: Mrs. Martha Freud
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mrs. Martha Freud

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones  

Mrs. Martha Freud—'Frau Professor' to a wide range of friends and acquaintances—died in London on November 2, 1951. It is fitting that this event should be recorded in our Journal, since she played an important, if unobtrusive, part in the development of psycho-analysis.

Martha Bernays was born in Hamburg on July 26, 1861. When she was eight years old her family moved to Vienna. There her brother became engaged to a sister of Freud's and married her in 1883. Freud met her through this connection in April, 1882, after which she and her mother returned to Hamburg. Freud was in no position to marry at the time, but after a daily correspondence and several visits to his fiancée, the wedding was at last celebrated on September 14, 1886, in Hamburg. She became the mother of six children, of whom five survive her.

Mrs. Freud was a devoted wife and, being unusually capable, was able to smooth her husband's life so as to give him the best opportunity for his own work. They went through hard and anxious times together, particularly in the first ten or fifteen years of the marriage. They celebrated their golden wedding three years before his death. A distinguished lady, her generous charm, her delightful hospitality, and her complete goodness of heart made her a notable figure. In the thirteen years she resided in London she became the centre of a wide circle of friends and relatives, who now sorely miss her.

60

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 1

Ernst Kris. 'Ego Psychology and Interpretation in Psychoanalytic Therapy.'

This paper begins with a brief historical résumé of the relationship between clinical observations and the development of analytical theory and technique. The point is particularly made that, whereas some theoretical advances influenced the content of interpretation without having any specific effect on technique, the influence of ego-psychology and the greater understanding of resistances afforded by it has led to a gradual transformation of technique. Resistance has ceased to be an obstacle to analysis and has become part of the 'psychic surface' which has to be explored.

Two clinical examples are given to demonstrate the way in which the technique of giving priority to interpretation of defence-mechanisms and of proceeding from the surface to the depths allows greater and more detailed understanding to be achieved. The second example shows how a second period of analysis based on this technique was able to supplement an earlier one by its greater concreteness, and by its greater success in linking past and present, adult symptomatology and infantile phantasy. It elucidated the defence-mechanisms used to inhibit activities, the unconscious origin of which had been correctly understood in the first analysis.

The paper ends with a discussion of planning and intuition, from the point of view of the way in which advances in ego-psychology have made possible more accurate teaching about the 'hierarchy' and 'timing' of interpretations. The view is expressed that the antithesis often assumed to exist between the two is false, and that the problem it attempted to clarify is spurious. The apparent choice between planning and intuition is really a matter of the individual analyst's preference for conscious or preconscious thought processes. All advances in analysis have come about as a result of their interaction.

74

responseSet.title: Clinical Notes on Internalization and Identification
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Notes on Internalization and Identification

Lois Munro

Lois Munro

Lois Munro
LONDON

Lois Munro  

In this paper I am concerned with the theme of internalization and identification as demonstrated in the analysis of a man of thirty-one years of age, an actor by profession. He presented a picture predominantly neurotic in character with compulsive features, but persecutory and depressive anxieties were present from the beginning. The analysis was incompiete, comprising seventeen months of analytic sessions, but extending over three and a half years. It was terminated for external reasons beyond the patient's control.

Melanie Klein has shown that from infancy onwards continuous processes of internalization take place leading to the building up within the subject's unconscious of an inner world peopled by objects: These are in part derived from real objects and in part from the subject's impulses and phantasies concerning them. Internalization is followed by identification. However, the nature of the identifications made by the subject are dependent upon a number of interacting factors of which anxieties, and defences against those anxieties, pertaining to the internal objects and their inter-relationship play a fundamental part. The depressive position is the central point in the ego's history and the way in which this is met determines the success or failure with which a whole, as opposed to a part, object is installed. It is my intention to show how the later identifications made by this man were determined and affected by the character of the early internalization of his first object, his mother.

The patient was married and had, at the time of his first visit, three children. A fourth child had died a few days after birth. For the first eighteen months he came on twelve separate occasions and only thereafter started a regular analysis. His behaviour during this earlier period varied; sometimes he would sit silent for the greater part of the hour, staring fixedly out of the window at the drain pipes of the opposite house; sometimes he would express himself in an extravagant manner that was characteristic of him. Thus, he said, at the outset and repeatedly throughout the analysis, he believed that in allowing himself to be seduced into marriage by his wife and being made the means of having brought four children into a brutal and war-stricken world, he had committed a crime of such magnitude that there was nothing to be done but to kill them all and himself. In this crime he had repeated, in spite of frequent self-warnings, the sins of his parents. There was a greater source of anxiety, over and above murder and suicide, and that was that he would become mad. Of one thing, he said, he was certain; he was sexually impotent and always had been. So uncompromising was this belief that it amounted to a delusion in view of the fact of his intercourse and the birth of four children. He could not for eighteen months bring himself to decide to have an analysis. He would ask me whether it could do him any good because after years of self-analysis he did not think so. He supposed he would have to be psycho-analysed in order to be psycho-analysed.

[[RunningHead]]

Psycho-Analysis and Ethics

R. Money-Kyrle

R. Money-Kyrle

R. E. Money-Kyrle
LONDON

R. Money-Kyrle  

Introductory Note

This paper was originally written (all but the last section in the spring of 1948) for readers with a general philosophic, rather than a specialized psycho-analytic, interest.1 In deciding to keep it for the special Birthday Number of this Journal, I had therefore to consider whether I should not redraft it for the other type of reader. But, as my aim was only to apply some of Melanie Klein's ideas in a philosophic field, to have gone into greater analytic detail would have tended to obscure my main argument. I hope this will explain, and excuse, my elementary and over-simplified treatment of analytic theory. The best account of the relevant part of such theory is to be found in Melanie Klein's own paper: 'A Contribution to the Theory of Anxiety and Guilt' (1948).

1. The Transfer of an Ethical Problem from Philosophy to Science

Philosophers are now divided into two main schools of thought: those who try to ask and answer metaphysical questions, and those who try to show that all metaphysical questions are meaningless.2 But if logic is on the side of the second school, we still need not dismiss all speculative philosophy as a sterile pursuit. The questions it formulated may often have been grammatically meaningless, but those who formulated them were clearly wrestling with some problem which they felt to be important. What was wrong was not that there was no problem, but that there was a failure to formulate it in such a way that an answer would be possible. So the essential difference between science and philosophy would seem to be, not that science deals with significant and philosophy with meaningless problems, but that science deals with those that are clear cut and philosophy with those which have not got beyond the stage of being only dimly felt.3 Many centuries of philosophical endeavour may be required before such questions get beyond this stage, and when they do they cease to be philosophical and are immediately transferred to science. In other words, the task of philosophy is perhaps always a preliminary one: that of formulating new problems for science.

responseSet.title: The Inner World in Ibsen's Master-Builder
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Inner World in Ibsen's Master-Builder

Joan Riviere

Joan Riviere

Joan Riviere
LONDON

Joan Riviere  

In my paper 'The Unconscious Phantasy of an Inner World reflected in Examples from English Literature'1 I brought together a few instances from poetry and prose in which a conscious awareness of containing other persons inside them, and of themselves existing in others, is more or less directly acknowledged by the writers. Here I have chosen a different kind of illustration of this theme, one in which conscious awareness of having beings inside one is not quite directly expressed, although it does appear in a special guise; nevertheless the whole meaning and content of the work has this and can have no other significance. It is a play; and the allusions in it to what is happening in the inner world are not occasional and transitory, nor are they elaborately overlaid by a realistic pattern of everyday life. The whole action and even the characters belong so predominantly to the inner world that the entire drama is a representation of that world; and moreover, to such a degree is this so that from the standpoint of external life the story seems to have no apparent motivation, and to some has even appeared ridiculous. The play is Ibsen's Master-Builder, whose meaning and message remains as mysterious to-day as it was when first performed more than fifty years ago, when nearly all the critics reacted to it with contempt.2 In spite of its insoluble and fantastic story, however, the play has from the beginning had a profound appeal, drawing audiences and moving them by powerful emotional forces in it which yet defy precise definition.

A full psycho-analysis of Ibsen's Master-Builder would be of great interest; here I shall give only a sketch of it and deal chiefly with the references in it to the inner world. The story is that of a builder, Solness, who has reached the pinnacle of success in his profession and has apparently attained everything or almost everything that life has to offer. In spite of being supreme, however, with his whole environment at his service, he is revealed as moody and unreasonable, overtly or tacitly harsh to all and preyed upon by anxiety. In particular he has intense feelings of insecurity in regard to the future, dread of being superseded by 'the younger generation', and he is haunted by the past. Alongside the story of his fears and his final fate, there is woven into the play the theme of daemonic forces influencing and impelling him both for and against his will. More than once Solness recounts solemnly, with awe and some uncertainty, that something he had 'wished and willed—silently and inwardly—' had come to pass; for instance, when he first met the young woman who was betrothed to his assistant he silently wished very ardently to attract her to him, to make a 'helper and server' of her—a phrase which is constantly on his lips—so as to keep the young man under him by her means; and then the next day she appeared, assuming she was to work for him as his book-keeper! In these connections casual references are made in the play to hypnotism or thought-transference, but no weight attaches to them. The weight of feeling lies in Solness's personal experience that some power in him which is not fully himself, but which he is responsible for, is at work on people round him. Conversely, he himself is restrained or impelled by an inner force, 'held back as by a hand' from some actions without understanding or strength to resist, driven and lured on to others.

Aspects of Symbolism in Comprehension of the Not-Self

Marion Milner

Marion Milner

Marion Milner
LONDON

Marion Milner  

Psycho-analytic Concepts of the Two Functions of the Symbol

Much has been written by psycho-analysts on the process by which the infant's interest is transferred from an original primary object to a secondary one. The process is described as depending upon the identification of the primary object with another that is in reality different from it but emotionally felt to be the same. Ernest Jones and Melanie Klein in particular, following up Freud's formulations, write about this transference of interest as being due to conflict with forces forbidding the interest in the original object, as well as to the actual loss of the original object. Jones, in his paper, 'The Theory of Symbolism' (1916), emphasizes the aspects of this prohibition which are to do with the forces that keep society together as a whole. Melanie Klein, in various papers, describes also the aspect of it which keeps the individual together as a whole; she maintains that it is the fear of our own aggression towards our original objects which makes us so dread their retaliation that we transfer our interest to less attacked and so less frightening substitutes. Jones also describes how the transfer of interest is due, not only to social prohibition and frustration and the wish to escape from the immanent frustrated mouth, penis, vagina and their retaliating counterparts, but also as due to the need to endow the external world with something of the self and so make it familiar and understandable.

The identification of one object with another is described as the forerunner of symbolism, and Melanie Klein, both in her paper 'Infant Analysis' (1923) and in the 'The Importance of Symbol Formation in the Development of the Ego' (1930), says that symbolism is the basis of all talents. Jones describes this identification as a process of symbolic equivalency through which progress to sublimation is achieved, but adds that symbolism itself, in the sense in which he uses the word, is a bar to progress. Leaving aside for a moment this difference over the use of the word symbol, there is one point about wording which, I do feel, requires comment.

responseSet.title: The Origins of Transference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Origins of Transference11

Melanie Klein

Melanie Klein

Melanie Klein
LONDON

Melanie Klein  

In his Fragment of an Analysis of a Case of Hysteria Freud defines the transference situation in the following way:—

What are transferences? They are new editions or facsimiles of the tendencies and phantasies which are aroused and made conscious during the progress of the analysis; but they have this peculiarity, which is characteristic for their species, that they replace some earlier person by the person of the physician. To put it another way: a whole series of psychological experiences are revived, not as belonging to the past, but as applying to the physician at the present moment.

In some form or other transference operates throughout life and influences all human relations, but here I am only concerned with the manifestations of transference in psycho-analysis. It is characteristic of psycho-analytic procedure that, as it begins to open up roads into the patient's unconscious, his past (in its conscious and unconscious aspects) is gradually being revived. Thereby his urge to transfer his early experiences, object-relations and emotions, is reinforced and they come to focus on the psycho-analyst; this implies that the patient deals with the conflicts and anxieties which have been reactivated, by making use of the same mechanisms and defences as in earlier situations.

It follows that the deeper we are able to penetrate into the unconscious and the further back we can take the analysis, the greater will be our understanding of the transference. Therefore a brief summary of my conclusions about the earliest stages of development is relevant to my topic.

The first form of anxiety is of a persecutory nature. The working of the death instinct within—which according to Freud is directed against the organism—gives rise to the fear of annihilation, and this is the primordial cause of persecutory anxiety. Furthermore, from the beginning of post-natal life (I am not concerned here with pre-natal processes) destructive impulses against the object stir up fear of retaliation. These persecutory feelings from inner sources are intensified by painful external experiences, for, from the earliest days onwards, frustration and discomfort arouse in the infant the feeling that he is being attacked by hostile forces. Therefore the sensations experienced by the infant at birth and the difficulties of adapting himself to entirely new conditions give rise to persecutory anxiety. The comfort and care given after birth, particularly the first feeding experiences, are felt to come from good forces. In speaking of 'forces' I am using a rather adult word for what the young infant dimly conceives of as objects, either good or bad. The infant directs his feelings of gratification and love towards the 'good' breast, and his destructive impulses and feelings of persecution towards what he feels to be frustrating, i.e. the 'bad' breast. At this stage splitting processes are at their height, and love and hatred as well as the good and bad aspects of the breast are largely kept apart from one another. The infant's relative security is based on turning the good object into an ideal one as a protection against the dangerous and persecuting object. These processes—that is to say splitting, denial, omnipotence and idealization—are prevalent during the first three or four months of life (which I termed the 'paranoid-schizoid position' (1946). In these ways at a very early stage persecutory anxiety and its corollary, idealization, fundamentally influence object relations.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Love and Hate11

Michael Balint

Michael Balint

Michael Balint
LONDON

Michael Balint  

I

If one has to ask the reader to re-examine some old, very familiar concepts, it is advisable to quote as an illustration a quite simple example. So I wish to begin with one which is an almost commonplace event in psycho-analytic practice. A patient of mine, a woman in her middle forties, recently bought a house, the first settled home in her much unsettled life. Of course, it was a great event; the house had to be altered, furnished and made just as she wanted it to be. I will not dwell on its obvious significance as a symbol of herself and behind that, of her mother. Anyhow it was a great thrill. Then she heard that a couple intended to visit her and to stay in the new house for about a fortnight. They were old, well-proved friends, and she was delighted that they were coming. They arrived, the house got ready just in time, and it was a great happiness. She could not repeat often enough how nice it was to have people one really loved as the first guests in a new house.

To our great surprise, within a few days, gradually, almost imperceptibly, feelings of irritation, tension, and uneasiness arose in her. The irritation increased till it amounted to a fairly severe anxiety state which, however, could be kept under control, though only with some difficulty. Gradually she became impatient: for heaven's sake, if only the people would leave! At this point some analysis became possible and we discovered behind the impatience and anxiety a bitter hatred against her 'friends'. As a result of this piece of analysis the anxiety subsided; the friends eventually departed, but the hatred against them remained practically unaltered.

Now this pattern was already well known to both of us. Anybody approaching her in friendship is equipped willy-nilly with 'angel wings' which (subjectively) means a blissful expectation that now she will really be loved, and she will be able to love safely. In this incident we discovered once again that it is impossible for anyone to live up to her expectations, that everybody must fall short of them because everybody has also his own life, needs, interests which are of necessity independent of, and so practically always different from, hers.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Notes on the Analysis of Sexual Perversions11

W. Gillespie

W. Gillespie

W. H. Gillespie, Dr.
LONDON

W. Gillespie  

In the Three Essays Freud uttered a famous aphorism which is sometimes treated as though it epitomizes all he has to say about the perversions. I refer to his statement that 'neuroses are, so to speak, the negative of perversions'. Now neurosis is, of course, in Freud's view, a compromise between a sexual impulse and its repudiation, and the statement, as it stands, suggests that the perversion represents the positive, unmodified sexual impulse, whose modification through defensive processes on the part of the ego gives rise to the neurosis. Thus it seems implied that in perversion we are dealing with an id activity, little interfered with by ego or superego. This at least is the interpretation put upon Freud's early discussion of perversion by many of the non-analytic critics. I need scarcely add that a general survey of Freud's writings on the perversions shows that this is a travesty of his view. In his discussions of homosexuality, of sado-masochism, of exhibitionism and voyeurism, and of fetishism Freud quite clearly credits the pervert with numerous other defence mechanisms besides regression, and obviously regards the ego as deeply involved in the process of perversion formation. One of the most interesting of these discussions is related to the splitting of the ego in fetishism, a splitting which is to be found, as he points out, not only in other perversions but in neuroses and psychoses as well.

The mechanism of splitting, both of the ego and of the object, has been discussed in detail by Melanie Klein; she relates it to denial, omnipotent idealization, and annihilation. Although she has described these as schizoid mechanisms, she clearly does not mean that they are to be found only in schizophrenics, but rather that they are amongst the earliest and most fundamental of the defence mechanisms of the ego. I have no doubt myself that splitting of the object and of the ego, denial and omnipotent manipulations of the relation to objects play a leading part in perversion formation and help us to understand its relationship to psychosis. Mrs. Klein suggests that in this early phase such mechanisms play a rôle similar to that of repression at a later stage. Here we have an important clue, I think, to the striking phenomenological differences between neurosis and perversion, which led Freud to say that the one is the negative of the other. In other words, we are dealing not with a contrast between defence and no defence, but between repressive defence and more primitive defence of a schizoid or splitting character.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference-Phenomena and Transference-Analysis in an Acute Catatonic Schizophrenic Patient

H. Rosenfeld

H. Rosenfeld

H. Rosenfeld
DR. LONDON

H. Rosenfeld  

SUMMARY

In this paper I have concentrated on certain aspects only of this case. I wished to illustrate that a severely ill withdrawn schizophrenic patient is capable of forming a positive and negative transference, that the transference can be observed and interpreted to the patient, and that the patient responded both to interpretations of the positive and negative transference. I also endeavoured to illustrate that the withdrawn state of the schizophrenic patient cannot be considered simply as an auto-erotic regression. The withdrawal of the schizophrenic may be a defence against external persecutors, or it may be due to identification with an object by both introjection and projection. In the state of identification which may be experienced by the patient as a confusion, the patient is aware of being mixed up with someone else (his object). Another purpose of this paper is to illustrate the regression of this schizophrenic patient to a stage of earliest infancy for which Melanie Klein has suggested the name 'Paranoid-Schizophrenic Position'. The patient shows signs of an object-relation, in which he has impulses and phantasies of forcing himself or parts of himself inside the object, which leads to states of confusion, to splitting of the self, to a loss of the self and to states of persecution in which visual and auditory hallucinations are pronounced.

This object-relation and the mechanisms developed from it and connected with it have an important bearing on many other neuroses and psychoses; they have a particular significance, however, for the whole group of the schizophrenias.

I suggest that the greater understanding of projective identification is beginning to open up a new field of research and in this paper I have attempted to show how this made it possible to understand and interpret the transference phenomena of this schizophrenic patient.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Biopsychical Aspects of Sado-Masochism11

Marie Bonaparte

Marie Bonaparte

Marie Bonaparte
PARIS

Marie Bonaparte  

We all know Freud's two theories of sado-masochism. The first, originally presented in Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality(1905) reads thus:

The most common and the most significant of all perversions— the desire to inflict pain upon the sexual object, and its reverse—received from Krafft-Ebing the names of 'sadism' and 'masochism' for its active and passive forms respectively. Other writers have preferred the narrower term 'algolagnia'. This emphasizes the pleasure in pain, the cruelty; whereas the names chosen by Krafft-Ebing bring into prominence the pleasure in any form of humiliation or subjection.

As regards active algolagnia, sadism, the roots are easy to detect in the normal. The sexuality of most male human beings contains an element of aggressiveness —and a desire to subjugate; the biological significance of it seems to lie in the need for overcoming the resistance of the sexual object by means other than the process of wooing. Thus sadism would correspond to an aggressive component of the sexual instinct which has become independent and exaggerated and, by displacement, has usurped the leading position.

In ordinary speech the connotation of sadism oscillates between, on the one hand, cases merely characterized by an active or violent attitude to the sexual object, and, on the other hand, cases in which satisfaction is entirely conditional on the humiliation and maltreatment of the object. Strictly speaking, it is only this last extreme instance which deserves to be described as a perversion.

Similarly, the term masochism comprises any passive attitude towards sexual life and the sexual object, the extreme instance of which appears to be that in which satisfaction is conditional upon suffering physical or mental pain at the hands of the sexual object. Masochism, in the form of a perversion, seems to be further removed from the normal sexual aim than its counterpart; it may be doubted at first sight whether it can ever occur as a primary phenomenon or whether, on the contrary, it may not invariably arise from a transformation of sadism. It can often be shown that masochism is nothing more than an extension of sadism turned round upon the subject's own self, which thus, to begin with, takes the place of the sexual object. Clinical analysis of extreme cases of masochistic perversion shows that a great number of factors (such as castration complex and the sense of guilt) have combined to exaggerate and fixate the original passive sexual attitude.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Notes on the Psycho-Analysis of the Super-Ego Conflict of an Acute Schizophrenic Patient

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld
LONDON

Herbert Rosenfeld  

SUMMARY

I have recorded a part of the analysis of this severely ill patient, not to claim a therapeutic success, but to help and encourage all those analysts who aim at treating schizophrenics by analysis, and who want to understand more about the psychopathology of schizophrenia. A detailed conscious understanding of the psychopathology of schizophrenics is, in my opinion, so important as this enables one to make full use of one's unconscious understanding of their utterances and behaviour in a transference analysis.

The problem of the super-ego and its development and origins is not only important for schizophrenia, but for the neuroses as a whole. Melanie Klein's research on the early origins of the super-ego, and the earliest anxieties, has been accepted by many but by no means all analysts. Some of the doubts they have about her view that these origins are to be found in earliest infancy arise from their difficulties in assessing the developmental period to which certain material belongs. It has frequently been suggested that the analysis of very young children, and of severely regressed schizophrenics, may help to throw further light on this problem.

I have tried to show that a transference analysis of a deeply regressed schizophrenic is possible, and that it can throw light on the earliest introjected objects and on their super-ego functions.

A Psycho-Analytical Approach to Aesthetics

Hanna Segal

Hanna Segal

Hanna Segal
LONDON

Hanna Segal  

Denn das Schöne ist nichts
als das Schrecklichen Anfang, den wir noch gerade
ertragen,
und wir bewundern es so, weil es gelassen
verschmäht
uns zu zerstören.

In 1908 Freud wrote: 'We laymen have always wondered greatly—like the cardinal who put the question to Ariosto—how that strange being, the poet, comes by his material. What makes him able to carry us with him in such a way and to arouse emotions in us of which we thought ourselves perhaps not even capable?'1 And as the science of psycho-analysis developed, repeated attempts were made to answer that question. Freud's discovery of unconscious phantasy life and of symbolism made it possible to attempt a psychological interpretation of works of art. Many papers have been written since, dealing with the problem of the individual artist and reconstructing his early history from an analysis of his work. The foremost of these is Freud's book on Leonardo da Vinci. Other papers have dealt with general psychological problems expressed in works of art showing, for instance, how the latent content of universal infantile anxieties is symbolically expressed in them. Such was Freud's paper 'The Theme of the Three Caskets', 2 Ernest Jones's 'The Conception of the Madonna through the Ear', 3 or Melanie Klein's 'Infantile Anxiety Situations Reflected in a Work of Art and the Creative Impulse.'4

Until recently such papers were not mainly concerned with æsthetics. They dealt with points of psychological interest but not with the central problem of æsthetics, which is: what constitutes good art, in what essential respect is it different from other human works, more particularly from bad art? Psychological writers attempted to answer questions like: 'How does the poet work?' 'What is he like?' 'What does he express?' In the paper 'The Relation of the Poet to Day-dreaming', Freud has shown how the work of the artist is a product of phantasy and has its roots, like the children's play and dreams, in unconscious phantasy life. But he did not attempt to explain 'why we should derive such pleasure from listening to the day-dreams of a poet'. How he achieves his effects is to Freud the poet's 'innermost secret'.5 Indeed, Freud was not especially interested in æsthetic problems. In 'The Moses of Michelangelo'6 he says: 'I have often observed that the subject-matter of works of art has a stronger attraction for me than their formal and technical qualities, though to the artist their value lies first and foremost in this latter. I am unable rightly to appreciate many of the methods used and the effects obtained in art.' He was also aware of the limitations of analytical theory in approaching æsthetics. In the preface to the book on Leonardo da Vinci7 he says that he has no intention of discussing why Leonardo was a great painter, since to do that, he would have to know more about the ultimate sources of the creative impulse and of sublimation. This was written in 1910. Since that time the work of Melanie Klein has thrown more light on the problem of the creative impulse and sublimation, and has provided a new stimulus to analytical writers on art. In the last fifteen years a number of papers have appeared dealing with problems of creation, beauty and ugliness.

Attempts during the last decade to reformulate psycho-analytical findings in propositions acceptable to experimental psychology showed promise that such reformulation might be most successful where concepts of learning theory were used. Psycho-analysts and psychologists both have grown to expect much from the 'multi-disciplinary approach', and one's expectations are therefore high when something like a text-book on the subject appears from the Yale Institute of Human Relations. This is a substantial, ambitious, provocative book (dedicated to 'Freud and Pavlov and their students') which the authors claim to have written from a 'joint Freudian-Behavioural approach' believed by them to be 'essential to the understanding of human social behaviour …'. Their aim has been '… to aid in the creation of a general science of human behaviour' by formulating '… a set of assumptions about human nature and personality which could be accepted by all the social sciences and humanities'. These result from 'a combination of three great disciplines—psycho-analysis, experimental psychology and sociology—into an integrated point of view'.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 20, 1951; No. 2

William N. Evans. 'Simulated Pregnancy in a Male.'

Leon Altman

Leon Altman

Leon Altman  

With the purpose of augmenting the sparse clinical contributions on this theme, this paper gives a case history account of a male's wish to bear a child. Evans describes a thirty-year-old man's re-enactment, during analysis, of his childhood theories of pregnancy and birth. His wish to have a child was acted out during treatment in hysterical seizures which imitated his conception of a witnessed parental intercourse. A marked tendency to reverse words, numbers, and images was in part determined by his having had two views of the primal scene, one reversed in a mirror. Repression and distortion of his scopophilia contributed to this tendency. In this analysis, he played out the drama of intercourse-pregnancy in reverse, a process which Freud described as a feature of the analysis of hysterical attacks. Abdominal pains, coughing and fear of bursting during delivery were dramatic features of his pregnancy fantasy. At the end of a forty-week gestation period he came to term in an unfruitful, and disappointing childbirth. In this, his identification with his mother was faithfully preserved even to significant dates. Pregnancy and childbirth also represented castration.

responseSet.title: A Contribution to the Re-Evaluation of the Oedipus Complex—the Early Stages
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Contribution to the Re-Evaluation of the Oedipus Complex—the Early Stages11

Paula Heimann

Paula Heimann

Paula Heimann
LONDON

Paula Heimann  

Introductory Remarks

For the purpose of this paper I have decided to concentrate on the early stages of the Oedipus complex which Melanie Klein has discovered in her analyses of young children. Her contributions naturally influence also the assessment of the later stages, but I think that the most useful way of approaching divergences of opinion lies in discussing the field in which they originate.

Although my presentation seems to emphasize the controversial points in our views on the Oedipus complex, this does not mean that we underrate the amount or the significance of the ground which we share.

Before dealing with my subject matter I wish to define our position with regard to some basic concepts and to outline briefly the period preceding the Oedipus complex.

The Theory of the Instincts

All understanding of psychological phenomena rests on Freud's discovery of the dynamic Unconscious. The two primary instincts of life and death, the borderland entities between soma and psyche, from which all instinctual impulses are derived, are the source of mental energy; all mental processes start from an unconscious stage.

Freud's concept of an inherent antithesis in the deepest and the most dynamic levels of the mind is fully born out by Melanie Klein's work. More than this, her work has produced many observations which substantiate his theory, but precisely on this account major divergences between her views and classical theory have arisen.

I am referring to the position which the mental facet of the instinctual urges, which we call 'unconscious phantasy', occupies in Melanie Klein's work.

Unconscious Phantasies

By the term unconscious phantasies we mean the most primitive psychic formations, inherent in the operation of the instinctual urges, and because these are inborn, we attribute unconscious phantasies to the infant from the beginning of his life. Unconscious phantasies occur not only in the infant, they are part of the unconscious mind at any time and form the matrix from which the pre-conscious and conscious processes develop. In the earliest stages they are almost the whole of psychical processes, and, of course, they are pre-verbal, or rather non-verbal. The words which we use when we wish to convey their contents and meaning, are a foreign element but we cannot do without it—unless we are artists.

responseSet.title: The Emotional Position of the Analyst in the Psycho-Analytic Situation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Emotional Position of the Analyst in the Psycho-Analytic Situation11

Maxwell Gitelson M.D.

Maxwell Gitelson

Maxwell Gitelson, M.D.
CHICAGO

Maxwell Gitelson  

I

The various clinical phenomena which have been called counter-transference occur in the setting of the analytic situation. The analytic situation may be described as the total configuration of interpersonal relationships and interpersonal events which evolve between the psycho-analyst and his patient. The patient's part in this configuration is understood in terms of the transference as discovered by Freud. The analyst's 'normal' rôle has been regarded from the same standpoint. It has been understood that the analyst so conducts himself that the transference in all its aspects develops in its own terms. Classical technique has been directed not towards its manipulation but towards its systematic resolution through analysis. This has been based on the assumption that the analyst is by his own intention and in the eyes of the patient a neutral figure on to whom the patient's centrally significant object relations are displaced.

Nevertheless, we have long been aware of deviations from this in practice. As early as 1910 Freud stated: 'We have begun to consider the "counter-transference", which arises in the physician as a result of the patient's influence on his unconscious feelings (my italics). … We have noticed that every analyst's achievement is limited by what his own complexes and resistances permit …' (1). In our students we have been able to identify 'errors in technique' as due not simply to ignorance, but often to intrusions from their own emotions and attitudes towards the patient. Even experienced analysts do not adhere strictly to theoretically prescribed conduct—sometimes with intention and sometimes for psychological reasons of their own. In all such instances there are results produced in the analytical situation which affect its configuration.

Furthermore, recent developments in the psychotherapeutic functions of psycho-analysis, such as experiences with borderline character problems (2) and with schizophrenic psychoses (3) have pointed to the importance of the analyst as a real object. It is important, therefore, to attempt a systematic consideration of the actual rôle of the analyst in the analytic situation.

responseSet.title: Group Dynamics: A Re-View
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Group Dynamics: A Re-View

W. Bion

W. Bion

W. R. Bion
LONDON

W. Bion  

Using knowledge derived from his psycho-analytic studies Freud attempted1 to illuminate some of the problems which had been raised by Le Bon, McDougall and others in their studies of the group. In this article I shall briefly summarize2 some theories at which I have arrived by applying to groups, in which I was participating, the intuitions developed by present-day psycho-analytic training. These theories differ from many others, in merits and defects alike, in having been forged in the actual emotional stresses they are intended to describe and they therefore constitute a different approach from that made by Freud. It will be noticed that I introduce some concepts new to psycho-analysis; this is in part because I am dealing with different subject-matter and in part because I wished to see if, by making a start disencumbered by previous theories, I might reach a point at which my views of the group and psycho-analytic views of the individual could be compared and thereby judged to be either complementary or divergent.

I assume that every individual acts as if he believes that the group has an attitude to him and that it is possible to put into words what this attitude is. Translation into precise speech of what I suppose to be the attitude of the group to me or to someone else, and of the individual to the group constitutes the interpretations.

The conclusions that follow represent the distillation of some eight years of work. These are they:

In any group there may be discerned certain broad trends of mental activity. Every group, however casual, however idle it may appear to be, meets to 'do' something. In this activity, according to the capacity of the individual, they co-operate. This co-operation is voluntary and dependent on some degree of sophisticated skill in the individual. Participation in this activity is a product of years of training, experience, and individual mental development. Since this activity is associated with the performance of a task it is related to reality, its methods are rational and therefore, in however embryonic a form, scientific. It follows that its characteristics are similar to those attributed by Freud to the Ego.3 This aspect of group mental activity I have called the Work Group (W).

responseSet.title: Freud's Conception of Love
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Conception of Love11

Edward Hitschmann M.D.

Edward Hitschmann

Edward Hitschmann, M.D.
CAMBRIDGE, MASS

Edward Hitschmann  

The time has gone when love was regarded as a mystery or as a visitation from God. We know that it is of libidinous origin and that it has a biological basis in that its basic aim is the sexual physical union of two people. But there is a special psychological aspect, which we also call love. It has been elaborated in famous love stories whose heroes and heroines are widely known and widely imitated in behaviour and speech. In romantic love, the most striking phenomenon is the unconscious overvaluation of the love object. It is this irrational component which justifies our scientific curiosity. To interpret the irrational is a task of our science.

What is the nature of love? What differentiates love, a fleeting infatuation, a sexual adventure, a marriage based on reason or on the admiration of a classic beauty?

This question, what is love, is infrequently posed, in spite of the fact that love is so universal a human experience. The average person accepts the phenomenon of love as uncritically as the phenomenon of sleep, just as he accepts the presence of religious faith, without inquiring into the reasons for its existence.

This paper will deal only with love in males.

Freud's explanation of the phenomena of love is contained chiefly in his Contributions to the Psychology of Love (1910, 1912, 1918) and Mass Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego (1921). Love, moreover, is discussed in the Collected Papers not less than about one-hundred and thirty times!

Among the phenomena of love we find many varieties. The common sensual love disappears temporarily following sexual gratification. The love object, however, may enjoy a persisting cathexis, and consequently is cherished or 'loved' also during desire-free periods. The tender longing is not aimed at sexuality, but merely originates in it. It is 'aim-inhibited'. Since it cannot bring full gratification followed by temporary withdrawal, it becomes the basis for the sustained aspects of a relationship.

According to Freud, it is the 'aim-inhibited instincts', the feeling of tenderness, which characterizes 'being in love'. These make their first appearance after the repression of oedipal wishes at the age of five or so. During puberty, general libidinous desires of the son directed towards the mother arise once more, again to be repressed and transformed. Tenderness, however, can be observed in much younger children in a fragmentary, somewhat dissociated form. It is possible that an element of imitation, of primitive identification with the tender loving parent-imago occurs.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The "King Oedipus" of Sophocles11

H. Van Der Sterren M.D.

H. Van Der Sterren

H. A. Van Der Sterren, M.D.
AMSTERDAM

H. Van Der Sterren  

In this paper I should like to give a summary and the main conclusions of a study I have dedicated to the three dramas of Sophocles: King Oedipus, Oedipus at Colonus, and Antigone. I have published this study in more detail as a Dutch book: De lotgevallen van Koning Oedipus ('The Fate of King Oedipus'), an English translation of which will, I hope, shortly appear. It is unfortunate that in this summary I must omit many details, since it is really these details which give life to the work as a whole.

It occurred to me to ask why these plays of Sophocles, from which Freud derived the name 'Oedipus complex', have not thus far been studied in detail, whereas many other literary works have been thoroughly analysed. There is a great difference between most other literary works and King Oedipus, for in the latter the oedipal emotions are openly discussed, while in the case of other works it is usually necessary to show that these emotions are expressed indirectly. This was my reason for writing about King Oedipus, for I intended to write also for non-psycho-analysts, and it therefore seemed to me of great importance that it was not necessary to deduce the very worst and most strongly repressed emotions, since these were clearly and precisely stated.

Apart from this I had a special question to put forward; since it was not necessary to demonstrate that the oedipal emotions are dealt with in King Oedipus, I could confine myself to a point of more interest to us, namely, the manner in which this theme is treated by Sophocles; what part of these emotions is expressed, how they are expressed, and how the defence mechanism functions. I therefore asked myself the same question which in my opinion must be asked in practical analysis, namely, What is the nature and form of the expressions and what is the defence? Of these it is, perhaps, the defence which is of greater interest and importance for us. How has this man assimilated the oedipal emotions, and what is the dynamic relationship in this case between the repressed and the defence? That there is an oedipal situation scarcely interests us, since we know that it exists and that it is the root of personality development.
